questions.specification: A random variable $X$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, such that $P(X < 30.31) = 0.1180$ and $P(X > 42.52) = 0.3060$.

parts.content: Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the cumulative probability for the upper boundary: $P(X < 42.52) = 1 - 0.3060 = 0.6940$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Set up standardizing equations for both boundaries: $P\left(Z < \frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 0.1180$ and $P\left(Z < \frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 0.6940$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Find the $z$-values from the standard normal distribution: $\frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma} = \Phi^{-1}(0.1180) = -1.1850...$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Find the second $z$-value: $\frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma} = \Phi^{-1}(0.6940) = 0.5072...$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Attempt to solve the linear equations simultaneously for $\mu$ and $\sigma$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the values: $\mu = 38.9$ and $\sigma = 7.22$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Convert to cumulative probabilities
steps.content:
To find the mean and standard deviation, we first need both given conditions to be in the form of cumulative probabilities, $P(X < x)$. 

The first condition is already cumulative:
$$P(X < 30.31) = 0.1180$$

For the second condition, we subtract the upper tail from $1$:
$$\begin{aligned} P(X < 42.52) &= 1 - P(X > 42.52) \\ &= 1 - 0.3060 \\ &= 0.6940 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
Using the inverse normal function on your calculator (with $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$), we find the $z$-scores corresponding to these cumulative probabilities.

For $P(Z < z_1) = 0.1180$:
$$z_1 \approx -1.1850$$

For $P(Z < z_2) = 0.6940$:
$$z_2 \approx 0.5072$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Probability menu, choose Distributions, then Inverse Normal. Input the areas 0.118 and 0.694 with mean 0 and standard deviation 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.118, μ: 0, σ: 1. Repeat for Area: 0.694.

desmos.instructions: Use the command inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.118) and inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.694).

calculator.description: Finding z-scores using the Inverse Normal function.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
The formula for a $z$-score is $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. We substitute our values to create a system of linear equations:

1.  $-1.1850 = \frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 30.31 - \mu = -1.1850\sigma$
2.  $0.5072 = \frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 42.52 - \mu = 0.5072\sigma$

Rearranging to isolate $\mu$:
$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 30.31 + 1.1850\sigma \\ \mu &= 42.52 - 0.5072\sigma \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for standard deviation
steps.content:
Set the two expressions for $\mu$ equal to each other to solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} 30.31 + 1.1850\sigma &= 42.52 - 0.5072\sigma \\ 1.1850\sigma + 0.5072\sigma &= 42.52 - 30.31 \\ 1.6922\sigma &= 12.21 \\ \sigma &= \frac{12.21}{1.6922} \\ \sigma &\approx 7.215... \end{aligned}$$

To three significant figures, **$\sigma = 7.22$**.

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\sigma$ back into one of our original equations to find $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 42.52 - 0.5072(7.215...) \\ \mu &\approx 38.859... \end{aligned}$$

To three significant figures, **$\mu = 38.9$**.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The parameters of the normal distribution are:
$$\mu = 38.9, \sigma = 7.22$$

steps.title: Standardize the probabilities
steps.content:
To solve for $\mu$ and $\sigma$, we first need to express both probabilities in terms of the area to the left ($P(X < x)$). 

The first probability is already in the correct form:
$$P(X < 30.31) = 0.1180$$

For the second probability, we are given the area to the right. We subtract from 1 to find the area to the left:
$$P(X < 42.52) = 1 - 0.3060 = 0.6940$$

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
Using the **Inverse Normal** function on your GDC (with mean $0$ and standard deviation $1$), find the $z$-scores corresponding to these cumulative probabilities.

For $P(Z < z_1) = 0.1180$:
$$z_1 \approx -1.1850$$

For $P(Z < z_2) = 0.6940$:
$$z_2 \approx 0.5072$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Normal -> Inverse Normal. Enter the area, μ=0, and σ=1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'invNorm(', enter Area: 0.118, μ: 0, σ: 1. Repeat for Area: 0.694.

desmos.instructions: Use the command: inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.118) and inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.694)

calculator.description: Calculate z-scores using the Inverse Normal function.

steps.title: Set up linear equations
steps.content:
We use the standardization formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, which can be rearranged to the linear form:
$$\mu + z\sigma = x$$

Substitute our values to create a system of two equations:
$$\begin{aligned} 1) \quad \mu - 1.1850\sigma &= 30.31 \\ 2) \quad \mu + 0.5072\sigma &= 42.52 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
Instead of solving by hand, we use the **Simultaneous Equation Solver** on the GDC. This tool takes the coefficients of our equations and calculates $\mu$ and $\sigma$ directly.

Input the coefficients into a $2 \times 3$ matrix or the solver tool:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1.1850 & 30.31 \\ 1 & 0.5072 & 42.52 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving this system gives:
$$\mu \approx 38.9$$
$$\sigma \approx 7.22$$

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Equation or Solver menu. Choose 'Simultaneous' for 2 unknowns. Enter the coefficients for both equations and press Solve.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Simult Eqn Solver'. Set to 2 equations and 2 unknowns. Enter the coefficients: 1, -1.1850, 30.31 and 1, 0.5072, 42.52. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: In separate lines, type the equations using 'm' for μ and 's' for σ: m - 1.1850s = 30.31 and m + 0.5072s = 42.52. Desmos will show the intersection.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations using the GDC's built-in solver.

steps.title: Identify knowns and task
steps.content: We are given a normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and two probability statements. Our goal is to find the mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$). Since both parameters are unknown, we will use the **Graphical Intersection Approach** by standardizing the values and solving the resulting equations graphically.

steps.title: Standardize the lower boundary
steps.content:
First, we look at the statement $P(X < 30.31) = 0.1180$. We use the standardization formula to convert $X$ to the standard normal variable $Z$:

$$\frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma} = z_1$$

Using the inverse normal function on your GDC with area $0.1180$, mean $0$, and standard deviation $1$, we find:

$$z_1 \approx -1.185039...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Inverse Normal. Enter Area: 0.118, Mean: 0, SD: 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.118, μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Use the inverse normal function.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for the lower boundary.

steps.title: Standardize the upper boundary
steps.content:
For the second statement $P(X > 42.52) = 0.3060$, we first convert it to a 'less than' probability to use the inverse normal function:

$$P(X < 42.52) = 1 - 0.3060 = 0.6940$$

Now, standardizing this value:

$$\frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma} = z_2$$

Using the GDC for area $0.6940$, we find:

$$z_2 \approx 0.507223...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Inverse Normal. Enter Area: 0.694, Mean: 0, SD: 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 1 - 0.306, μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Use the inverse normal function.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for the upper boundary.

steps.title: Rearrange for graphing
steps.content:
To find the intersection, we rearrange both equations to express $\mu$ as a function of $\sigma$ (letting $y = \mu$ and $x = \sigma$ for the GDC).

From the first equation:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma} &= -1.185039 \\ 30.31 - \mu &= -1.185039\sigma \\ \mu &= 1.185039\sigma + 30.31 \end{aligned}$$

From the second equation:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma} &= 0.507223 \\ 42.52 - \mu &= 0.507223\sigma \\ \mu &= -0.507223\sigma + 42.52 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the intersection point
steps.content:
Now, graph both linear functions on your GDC. We look for the point where the two lines cross. This point $(x, y)$ represents $(\sigma, \mu)$.

- Function 1: $y = 1.185039x + 30.31$
- Function 2: $y = -0.507223x + 42.52$

Using the **Intersect** tool on your calculator, you will find:

$$x \approx 7.2151...$$
$$y \approx 38.8602...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Graphing menu. Enter the two equations. Use G-Solv (F5) and select INTSECT (F5).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter Y1 = 1.185039X + 30.31 and Y2 = -0.507223X + 42.52. Press [GRAPH]. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 5:intersect. Press [ENTER] three times.

desmos.instructions: Enter the two equations and click on the grey dot representing the intersection point.

calculator.description: Graph the two lines and find where they intersect.

steps.title: Finalize the results
steps.content:
Rounding the intersection values to three significant figures, we get our final answers:

$$\mu = 38.9$$
$$\sigma = 7.22$$

parts.content: Find $P(|X - \mu| < 1.2\sigma)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Express the probability in terms of the standard normal variable $Z$: $P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the resulting probability: $0.770$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award **M1A1** for $P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2) = 0.770$.</note>

steps.title: Interpret the absolute value
steps.content:
The question asks us to find $P(|X - \mu| < 1.2\sigma)$.

In mathematics, an absolute value inequality like $|a| < b$ is equivalent to $-b < a < b$. Applying this to our expression:

$$-1.2\sigma < X - \mu < 1.2\sigma$$

steps.title: Transform to Standard Normal
steps.content:
To solve this using the standard normal distribution ($Z$), we use the transformation formula:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

By dividing all parts of our inequality by the standard deviation $\sigma$, we transform the variable $X$ into $Z$:

$$\frac{-1.2\sigma}{\sigma} < \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} < \frac{1.2\sigma}{\sigma}$$

This simplifies to finding the probability:

$$P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2)$$

This step earns you the first mark (**M1**) for identifying the Z-score range.

steps.title: Calculate using technology
steps.content: Now, we use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to find the area under the standard normal curve between $-1.2$ and $1.2$. For the standard normal distribution, we use a mean of $0$ and a standard deviation of $1$.

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the Statistics menu, then Dist -> NORM -> Ncd. Set Lower to -1.2, Upper to 1.2, σ to 1, and μ to 0.

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 2:normalcdf(. Enter the lower bound, upper bound, mean (0), and standard deviation (1).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1).cdf(-1.2, 1.2)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2) using the normal cumulative distribution function.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
From the calculator, we get:

$$P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2) \approx 0.76986...$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we obtain:

$$P(|X - \mu| < 1.2\sigma) = 0.770$$

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
To solve for $\mu$ and $\sigma$, we first find the $z$-scores corresponding to the given probabilities using the inverse normal function on our calculator.

For $P(X < 30.31) = 0.1180$:
$$z_1 = \text{invNorm}(0.1180, 0, 1) \approx -1.18503$$

For $P(X > 42.52) = 0.3060$, which is equivalent to $P(X < 42.52) = 1 - 0.3060 = 0.6940$:
$$z_2 = \text{invNorm}(0.6940, 0, 1) \approx 0.50722$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Inverse Normal function with Mean 0 and Standard Deviation 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.1180, μ: 0, σ: 1. Repeat for Area: 1 - 0.3060.

desmos.instructions: Use the inverse cumulative probability function for the normal distribution.

calculator.description: Find the z-scores for the given probabilities.

steps.title: Establish the linear equations
steps.content:
Using the $z$-score formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, we can set up a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad -1.18503 &= \frac{30.31 - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow 30.31 = \mu - 1.18503\sigma \\ (2) \quad 0.50722 &= \frac{42.52 - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow 42.52 = \mu + 0.50722\sigma \end{aligned}$$

This uses concepts of modeling with systems of equations from **Topic 1.8**.

steps.title: Solve for parameters
steps.content:
Subtracting equation (1) from equation (2) to eliminate $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} 42.52 - 30.31 &= (0.50722\sigma) - (-1.18503\sigma) \\ 12.21 &= 1.69225\sigma \\ \sigma &= \frac{12.21}{1.69225} \approx 7.2152 \end{aligned}$$

Now, substitute $\sigma$ back into equation (2) to find $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 42.52 - 0.50722(7.2152) \\ \mu &\approx 38.860 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Solve the system of linear equations using basic arithmetic.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the difference in x values and divide by the difference in z-scores.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value for sigma.

calculator.description: Calculate the values of sigma and mu.

steps.title: Define the target probability
steps.content:
We need to find $P(|X - \mu| < 1.2\sigma)$. We can expand this absolute value inequality:

$$\begin{aligned} -1.2\sigma &< X - \mu < 1.2\sigma \\ \mu - 1.2\sigma &< X < \mu + 1.2\sigma \end{aligned}$$

Using our calculated values for $\mu$ and $\sigma$:
Lower bound: $38.860 - 1.2(7.2152) \approx 30.202$
Upper bound: $38.860 + 1.2(7.2152) \approx 47.518$

steps.title: Calculate the final probability
steps.content:
Now we use the **Normal CDF** function on our calculator with our specific parameters $X \sim N(38.860, 7.2152^2)$ to evaluate the probability:

$$P(30.202 < X < 47.518) \approx 0.770$$

As shown in the markscheme, this is mathematically equivalent to calculating $P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2)$ for the standard normal distribution.

gdc.instructions: Use the Normal Cumulative Distribution function with the specified mean and standard deviation.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 2:normalcdf(. Enter lower: 30.202, upper: 47.518, μ: 38.860, σ: 7.2152.

desmos.instructions: Use the normal distribution function and find the probability between the bounds.

calculator.description: Find the area under the normal curve between the calculated bounds.

highlights.text: Express the probability in terms of the standard normal variable $Z$: $P(-1.2 < Z < 1.2)$

questions.specification:
The times taken for male runners to complete a marathon can be modelled by a normal distribution with a mean of 196 minutes and a standard deviation of 24 minutes.

It is found that 5% of the male runners complete the marathon in less than $T_1$ minutes.

The times taken for female runners to complete the marathon can be modelled by a normal distribution with a mean of 210 minutes. It is found that 58% of female runners complete the marathon between 185 and 235 minutes.

parts.content: Find the probability that a male runner selected at random will complete the marathon in less than 3 hours.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify the distribution and parameters: $T \sim N(196, 24^2)$
  • Calculate the probability for $T < 180$ (3 hours): $P(T < 180)$ M1
  • State the final answer: $0.252$ A1

2 marks total

steps.title: Convert hours to minutes
steps.content:
The mean and standard deviation are given in minutes, so we first need to convert the time limit of 3 hours into minutes to ensure our units match.

$$3 \text{ hours} = 3 \times 60 \text{ minutes} = 180 \text{ minutes}$$

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
We are told the times follow a normal distribution. Let $T$ be the time taken by a male runner. We can write this as:

$$T \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

$$T \sim N(196, 24^2)$$

We want to find the probability that a runner takes less than 180 minutes, which is written as $P(T < 180)$.

highlights.text: normal distribution with a mean of 196 minutes and a standard deviation of 24 minutes

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content:
Using the Direct Calculator Method, we use the normal cumulative distribution function (**normCDF**) on our calculator. 

We set our boundaries as:
- **Lower bound**: $-\infty$ (or a very small number like $-10^{99}$)
- **Upper bound**: $180$
- **Mean** ($\mu$): $196$
- **Standard deviation** ($\sigma$): $24$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Statistics or Probability menu.
2. Select Distributions -> Continuous -> Normal C.D.F.
3. Enter Lower: -1E99, Upper: 180, μ: 196, σ: 24.
4. Execute to find the probability.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR).
2. Select 2: normalcdf(.
3. Enter the following values:
   - lower: -1E99
   - upper: 180
   - μ: 196
   - σ: 24
4. Select Paste and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type 'normaldist(196, 24).cdf(0, 180)' into a cell.
2. Note: For time, we can use 0 as the lower bound instead of -infinity.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability of a value being less than 180 in a normal distribution.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
From the calculator, we find:

$$P(T < 180) \approx 0.252492...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:

$$P(T < 180) = 0.252$$

steps.title: Convert units and identify parameters
steps.content:
The question provides the mean and standard deviation in minutes, but the target time is given in hours. We must first convert 3 hours into minutes so the units are consistent.

$$3 \text{ hours} = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ minutes}$$

We are given the following parameters for the normal distribution of male runners, $M$:
- Mean $(\mu) = 196$
- Standard deviation $(\sigma) = 24$
- $M \sim N(196, 24^2)$

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
To use the standardization method, we transform our value $x = 180$ into a $z$-score. The $z$-score represents how many standard deviations a value is from the mean.

Using the formula:
$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} z &= \frac{180 - 196}{24} \\ z &= \frac{-16}{24} \\ z &\approx -0.667 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the probability using technology
steps.content:
We now need to find the probability that a runner takes less than 3 hours, which is $P(M < 180)$. In terms of the standard normal distribution, this is $P(Z < -0.666...)$.

Using a GDC, we find the area under the standard normal curve to the left of our $z$-score.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Distribution menu, select Normal Cdf. Set Lower Bound: -9999, Upper Bound: -0.666667, Mean: 0, SD: 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'normalcdf'. Set 'lower' to -1E99, 'upper' to -0.666667, 'μ' to 0, and 'σ' to 1. Select 'Paste' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1).cdf(-infinity, -0.666667)' into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability for the standard normal distribution using the z-score.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the calculation:
$$P(Z < -0.666...) \approx 0.252492...$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:
$$P(M < 180) = 0.252$$

This represents a $25.2\%$ chance that a male runner finishes in less than 3 hours.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
From the question, we know the marathon completion times for male runners follow a normal distribution. We are given the mean ($\mu$) and the standard deviation ($\sigma$):

$$\mu = 196$$
$$\sigma = 24$$

steps.title: Set up probability equation
steps.content:
We are looking for the time $T_1$ such that $5\%$ of runners complete the race in less time. This means the probability of a runner finishing in less than $T_1$ minutes is $0.05$.

$$\mathrm{P}(T < T_1) = 0.05$$

highlights.text: 5% of the male runners complete the marathon in less than $T_1$ minutes

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
To solve this using standardization, we first find the $z$-score from the standard normal distribution $Z \sim \mathrm{N}(0, 1)$ that corresponds to a left-tail area of $0.05$.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator:
$$\mathrm{P}(Z < z) = 0.05 \Rightarrow z \approx -1.645$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics or Probability menu, select Distributions, then Inverse Normal. Set Area = 0.05, Mean = 0, and SD = 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.05, μ: 0, σ: 1, Tail: LEFT. Press Paste and ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type `inverseNormal(normaldist(0,1), 0.05)` into a cell.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for the 5th percentile using the Inverse Normal function.

steps.title: Apply standardization formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standardization formula to relate our specific distribution to the standard normal distribution:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substitute the known values ($z \approx -1.64485$, $\mu = 196$, and $\sigma = 24$) to solve for $T_1$:

$$\begin{aligned} -1.64485 &= \frac{T_1 - 196}{24} \\ T_1 - 196 &= -1.64485 \times 24 \\ T_1 &= 196 + (-39.4764) \\ T_1 &= 156.5236... \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression 196 + (-1.64485 * 24) on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the final value for T1.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures, we get:

$$T_1 = 157 \text{ minutes}$$

parts.content: Find the standard deviation of the times taken by female runners.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify the distribution: $F \sim N(210, \sigma^2)$
  • Recognize symmetry or calculate upper tail probability: $P(F < 235) = 0.79$ M1
  • Set up the standardized equation: $\frac{235 - 210}{\sigma} = 0.806421\ldots$ M1A1
  • Calculate the standard deviation: $\sigma = 31.0$ A1

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify the distribution
steps.content:
From the question, we know the times for female runners, $F$, follow a normal distribution with a mean $\mu = 210$. We are also given that $58\%$ of female runners complete the marathon between $185$ and $235$ minutes.

$$F \sim N(210, \sigma^2)$$
$$P(185 < F < 235) = 0.58$$

highlights.text: 58% of female runners complete the marathon between 185 and 235 minutes

steps.title: Find the tail probability
steps.content:
The normal distribution is symmetric about the mean. Notice that the values $185$ and $235$ are both exactly $25$ minutes away from the mean ($210 - 185 = 25$ and $235 - 210 = 25$).

Since $58\%$ of the data is in the middle, the remaining $42\%$ must be split equally between the two tails. Each tail contains:

$$\text{Tail probability} = \frac{1 - 0.58}{2} = 0.21$$

To find the cumulative probability up to $235$ minutes, we add the lower tail to the middle section:

$$P(F < 235) = 0.21 + 0.58 = 0.79$$

steps.title: Determine the z-score
steps.content:
We now need to find the $z$-score on the standard normal distribution $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ that corresponds to a cumulative probability of $0.79$. 

Using the inverse normal function on your GDC:

$$P(Z < z) = 0.79 \Rightarrow z \approx 0.806421\dots$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Inverse Normal function with area 0.79, mean 0, and standard deviation 1.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd VARS (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.79, μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Use the normal distribution function with inverse CDF.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for an area of 0.79 using inverse normal functions.

steps.title: Solve for standard deviation
steps.content:
We can now use the standardization formula to relate the real-world value $x = 235$ to our $z$-score:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substitute the known values ($z = 0.806421\dots$, $x = 235$, $\mu = 210$) and solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.806421 &= \frac{235 - 210}{\sigma} \\ 0.806421 &= \frac{25}{\sigma} \\ \sigma &= \frac{25}{0.806421} \\ \sigma &\approx 31.0 \end{aligned}$$

The standard deviation for female runners is $31.0$ minutes (to 3 significant figures).

steps.title: Identify the distribution

steps.title: State the probability equation

We are given that 58% of female runners finish between 185 and 235 minutes. We can write this as a probability equation:

To solve for $\sigma$, we need to input the cumulative normal distribution function into the solver. On a GDC, the cumulative normal distribution is usually represented as `normalcdf(lower, upper, mean, sigma)`.

We want to find $x$ (which represents $\sigma$) such that:

$$\text{normalcdf}(185, 235, 210, x) - 0.58 = 0$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the GDC numerical solver, we find that the value of $\sigma$ is approximately $31.0$.

In IB exams, you should always round your final answer to 3 significant figures unless stated otherwise.

parts.content: Find the value of $k$.
parts.markscheme:
<note>This question is from an exam for a previous syllabus, and may contain minor differences in marking or structure.</note>

- Attempt to equate integral to 1 (may appear later): $k \displaystyle\int_0^6 \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \text{d}x = 1$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct integration: $k \left[ -\dfrac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_0^6 = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute limits: $-\dfrac{6}{\pi}(-1 - 1) = \dfrac{1}{k}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State final answer: $k = \dfrac{\pi}{12}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Use the PDF property
steps.content:
For any continuous random variable, the total area under the probability density function (PDF) must equal 1. This represents the total probability of all possible outcomes. We can express this using a definite integral over the given range $[0, 6]$:

$$\int_{0}^{6} f(x) \, dx = 1$$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
Substitute the given function into our integral equation. Since $k$ is a constant, we can factor it out of the integral:

$$k \int_{0}^{6} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \, dx = 1$$

steps.title: Apply reverse chain rule
steps.content:
To integrate $\sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$, we use the reverse chain rule. 

Recall that $\int \sin(ax) \, dx = -\frac{1}{a} \cos(ax) + C$. 

In our case, $a = \frac{\pi}{6}$, so its reciprocal is $\frac{6}{\pi}$. The integral becomes:

$$k \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{0}^{6} = 1$$

steps.title: Substitute the limits
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the expression at the upper limit ($x=6$) and subtract the value at the lower limit ($x=0$):

$$\begin{aligned} k \left[ \left( -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi \cdot 6}{6} \right) \right) - \left( -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi \cdot 0}{6} \right) \right) \right] &= 1 \\ k \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos(\pi) + \frac{6}{\pi} \cos(0) \right] &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

Using known trig values, $\cos(\pi) = -1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} k \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi}(-1) + \frac{6}{\pi}(1) \right] &= 1 \\ k \left[ \frac{6}{\pi} + \frac{6}{\pi} \right] &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for k
steps.content:
Combine the terms and solve the linear equation for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} k \left( \frac{12}{\pi} \right) &= 1 \\ k &= \frac{\pi}{12} \end{aligned}$$

This exact value ensures the total area under the curve is exactly 1.

gdc.instructions: Use the numerical integration tool to calculate the area under sin(πx/6) from 0 to 6.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] [9] (fnInt). Enter the integral: fnInt(sin(πX/6), X, 0, 6). Ensure your calculator is in RADIAN mode.

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral symbol. Set the bounds from 0 to 6 and the function as sin(πx/6).

calculator.description: Verify the value of the integral (without k) to ensure the arithmetic is correct.

steps.title: Set total area to 1
steps.content:
A fundamental property of any **probability density function** (PDF) is that the total area under the curve over its entire domain must equal 1. For this function, the domain where it is non-zero is from $x = 0$ to $x = 6$.

Therefore, we set up the following equation:
$$\int_{0}^{6} k \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = 1$$

steps.title: Isolate the constant $k$
steps.content:
Since $k$ is a constant, we can move it outside of the integral. This allows us to focus on calculating the numerical value of the integral first:

$$k \times \int_{0}^{6} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = 1$$

steps.title: Evaluate integral using GDC
steps.content:
Now, use your graphing calculator to find the numerical value of $\int_{0}^{6} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$. 

**Note:** Ensure your calculator is in **Radian mode** whenever you are integrating trigonometric functions in IB Math.

From the calculator, we find:
$$\int_{0}^{6} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx \approx 3.8197...$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Run-Matrix menu.
2. Press [OPTN], then [F4] (CALC).
3. Press [F4] (∫dx).
4. Input sin(πx/6), 0, 6.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH].
2. Select 9:fnInt(.
3. Enter the expression: fnInt(sin(πX/6), X, 0, 6).
4. Ensure mode is in RADIANS.

desmos.instructions: Type 'int', then fill in the bounds (0 to 6) and the function sin(pi*x/6).

calculator.description: Calculate the definite integral of sin(pi*x/6) from 0 to 6.

steps.title: Solve for $k$
steps.content:
Substitute the integral value back into our equation from Step 1:

$$\begin{aligned} k \times 3.819718634 &= 1 \\ k &= \frac{1}{3.819718634} \\ k &\approx 0.261799... \end{aligned}$$

While the numerical answer is $\approx 0.262$, we can see from the calculation $\frac{12}{\pi} \approx 3.8197$ that the exact value is $k = \frac{\pi}{12}$. Both forms are acceptable, but the exact value is often preferred.

$$k = \frac{\pi}{12}$$

steps.title: Analyze the function's shape
steps.content:
The probability density function (PDF) is given as:

$$f(x) = k \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right), \text{ for } 0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$$

In the IB curriculum, we know that the mean of a continuous random variable represents the "balance point" of the distribution. If a PDF is perfectly symmetric over its domain, the mean is simply the midpoint of that domain.

steps.title: Recognize the symmetry
steps.content:
Let's look at the argument of the sine function: $\frac{\pi x}{6}$.

*   When $x = 0$, the argument is $0$.
*   When $x = 6$, the argument is $\pi$.

The sine function forms a single, symmetric arch between $0$ and $\pi$. Since our domain $[0, 6]$ maps perfectly to this arch, the graph of $f(x)$ is symmetric about the middle of the interval.

steps.title: Identify the midpoint
steps.content:
Because the graph is symmetric over the interval $[0, 6]$, we can "write down" the mean by finding the midpoint:

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{0 + 6}{2}$$

$$\text{Mean} = 3$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the first cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the interval [0, 6].

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: By observing the symmetry of the sine curve over the given domain, we conclude that the mean of $X$ is $3$.

steps.title: Analyze the Distribution Shape
steps.content: The probability density function $f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ is a sinusoidal curve on the interval $[0, 6]$. Since a sine wave is perfectly symmetric between its intercepts (where the value is 0), we can determine the mean by finding the center of this symmetry, which corresponds to the **mode** (the highest point of the graph).

steps.title: Locate the Mode
steps.content:
A sine function $\sin(\theta)$ reaches its maximum value when its argument $\theta$ is equal to $\frac{\pi}{2}$. To find the $x$-coordinate of the peak (the mode), we set the argument of our function equal to $\frac{\pi}{2}$:

$$\frac{\pi x}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Solving for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{\pi}{2} \times \frac{6}{\pi} \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (pi/2) * (6/pi).

ti84.instructions: Solve the equation (pi*X)/6 = pi/2 by multiplying both sides by 6/pi.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression.

steps.title: Relate Mode to Mean
steps.content: For a symmetric unimodal (single-peaked) distribution, the mean, median, and mode are all located at the same point. Since the graph of $f(x)$ is a single sine hump that is symmetric about its maximum at $x = 3$, the mean must also be at this location.

steps.title: State the Final Answer
steps.content:
By observing the symmetry of the sine curve over the interval $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$, the mean of $X$ is:

$$\text{Mean} = 3$$

steps.title: Identify the Symmetry
steps.content:
When you look at the graph on your calculator, you will see a single 'hump' of a sine wave starting at $x=0$ and returning to the x-axis at $x=6$. 

Because the sine function is perfectly symmetrical, the highest point and the center of the area occur exactly halfway between the boundaries. 

$$\text{Mean} = \frac{0 + 6}{2} = 3$$

Since the distribution is symmetrical about $x=3$, the mean (expected value) must be $3$.

steps.title: Understand the Median
steps.content:
For a continuous random variable, the **median** is the value $m$ that divides the area under the probability density function (PDF) exactly in half. This means:

$$\int_{0}^{m} f(x) \, dx = 0.5$$

Instead of calculating this integral, we can determine the median by looking at the symmetry of the graph of $f(x)$.

steps.title: Analyze Graph Symmetry
steps.content:
The function is defined as $f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ for $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$. 

Notice that the sine function creates a single "hump" over this interval:
- When $x = 0$, $f(0) = k \sin(0) = 0$.
- When $x = 6$, $f(6) = k \sin(\pi) = 0$.

The sine function is perfectly symmetrical about its peak. The peak occurs at the midpoint of the interval $[0, 6]$.

steps.title: Identify the Bisecting Line
steps.content:
Since the graph is symmetrical on the interval $[0, 6]$, the vertical line that passes through the center will bisect the total area. 

The center of the interval is calculated as:

$$x = \frac{0 + 6}{2} = 3$$

Because the line $x = 3$ is the axis of symmetry, exactly $50\%$ of the area lies to the left of $x = 3$, and $50\%$ lies to the right.

steps.title: State the Median
steps.content:
By the property of symmetry in a probability density function, the median is located at the axis of symmetry.

$$\text{Median} = 3$$

steps.title: Analyze the distribution shape
steps.content:
The probability density function (PDF) is defined as $f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ for $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$. 

This function represents a single "arch" or "hump" of a sine curve. Since a standard sine wave is perfectly symmetrical about its peak, we can determine the median by finding the line of symmetry for this distribution.

steps.title: Locate the peak (Mode)
steps.content:
For a unimodal (single-peaked) symmetric distribution, the median is located exactly at the same $x$-coordinate as the peak (the mode).

The maximum value of a sine function $\sin(\theta)$ occurs when the angle $\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}$. To find the $x$-value of the peak, we set the argument of our sine function to $\frac{\pi}{2}$:

$$\frac{\pi x}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi$ and multiplying by $6$ gives us:

$$x = \frac{6}{2} = 3$$

steps.title: State the median
steps.content:
Because the curve is perfectly symmetrical between $x = 0$ and $x = 6$, the area is divided exactly in half at the center point. 

As we identified in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**, specifically under central tendency (**SL 4.3**), the median is the value that splits the data into two equal halves. For this symmetric sine curve, that point is the peak.

$$\text{Median} = 3$$

steps.title: Understand the median condition
steps.content:
The **median** $m$ of a continuous random variable is the value where the cumulative probability is exactly $0.5$. In terms of the area under the curve (the integral), this means half of the total probability lies to the left of $m$:

$$\int_{0}^{m} f(x) \, dx = 0.5$$

Since this is a "write down" question worth 1 mark, we are looking for a feature of the graph that makes this calculation simple.

highlights.text: write down the median of X

steps.title: Analyze the graph's symmetry
steps.content:
The probability density function is $f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ for $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$. 

Recall that a sine function $\sin(\theta)$ is symmetric about its peak at $\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}$. For our function:
- At $x=0$, the argument is $0$.
- At $x=6$, the argument is $\pi$.
- The peak occurs exactly in the middle at $x=3$, where the argument is $\frac{\pi(3)}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$.

Because the sine curve is perfectly symmetrical between $x=0$ and $x=6$, the area is divided into two identical halves by the line $x=3$.

steps.title: Verify with integration
steps.content:
Using the **Analytical Integration** approach, we can verify that $x=3$ splits the area in half. First, we would find $k$ such that the total area is $1$. Then, we check the area from $0$ to $3$:

$$\int_{0}^{3} k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \, dx = 0.5$$

Due to the symmetry we identified in the graph, we know that:
$$\int_{0}^{3} f(x) \, dx = \int_{3}^{6} f(x) \, dx$$

Since the total area is $1$, each of these integrals must equal $0.5$.

steps.title: Define the mode
steps.content: In statistics, the **mode** of a continuous random variable $X$ is the value of $x$ where the probability density function $f(x)$ reaches its maximum value. Visually, this is the highest point (the peak) on the graph of the function.

steps.title: Analyze the sine arch
steps.content:
The function is given by:

$$f(x) = k \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$

Notice the interval $0 \le x \le 6$. Let's look at the behavior of the sine term:
- When $x = 0$, the angle is $0$, and $\sin(0) = 0$.
- When $x = 6$, the angle is $\frac{6\pi}{6} = \pi$, and $\sin(\pi) = 0$.

Because the sine function creates a single positive "arch" between $0$ and $\pi$, the graph of $f(x)$ is a single symmetrical hump starting at $x=0$ and ending at $x=6$.

steps.title: Use symmetry for midpoint
steps.content:
A sine arch is perfectly symmetrical. The maximum value (the peak) occurs exactly halfway between the two points where the function is zero ($x=0$ and $x=6$).

To find the midpoint, we calculate the average:

$$\text{Midpoint} = \frac{0 + 6}{2} = 3$$

Thus, the peak of the graph occurs at $x = 3$.

steps.title: Define the mode
steps.content: For a continuous random variable, the **mode** is the value of $x$ where the probability density function $f(x)$ reaches its maximum value. To find this peak mathematically, we can look for the stationary point where the gradient of the function is zero.

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
We differentiate $f(x)$ with respect to $x$ using the chain rule. This falls under **AHL Topic 5.9: Differentiating standard functions**.

Given:
$$f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$

The derivative is:
$$f'(x) = k \cdot \cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{6}$$

Simplifying this, we get:
$$f'(x) = \dfrac{k\pi}{6} \cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$

steps.title: Find the stationary point
steps.content:
To find the maximum, we set the derivative equal to zero:

$$\dfrac{k\pi}{6} \cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) = 0$$

Since $k$ and $\pi$ are constants (and $k \neq 0$ for a valid PDF), we only need to solve for when the cosine term is zero:

$$\cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve the trigonometric equation
steps.content:
We need to find the value of $x$ in the interval $[0, 6]$ that satisfies the equation. We know that $\cos(\theta) = 0$ when $\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}$ (for the first quadrant).

$$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{\pi x}{6} &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\ x &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{6}{\pi} \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

Since $x=3$ is within the domain $0 \le x \le 6$, this is our candidate for the mode.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The derivative calculation shows that the function $f(x)$ has a stationary point at $x = 3$. Because the sine function $\sin(\frac{\pi x}{6})$ starts at $0$ (at $x=0$), increases to its maximum, and returns to $0$ (at $x=6$), this stationary point must be a maximum.

Therefore, the mode of $X$ is $3$.

parts.content: Show that $P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \dfrac{1}{4}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to set up the integral: $\dfrac{\pi}{12} \displaystyle\int_0^2 \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \text{d}x$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct integration: $= \dfrac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\dfrac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_0^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute limits: $\dfrac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\dfrac{6}{\pi} \left( \cos \dfrac{\pi}{3} - 1 \right) \right]$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Show the result: $= \dfrac{1}{4}$ <marks>AG</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Accept without the $\frac{\pi}{12}$ at the first stage if it is added later.</note>

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the probability $P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2)$, we evaluate the definite integral of the probability density function $f(x)$ over the interval $[0, 2]$. 

From previous work, we know that $k = \dfrac{\pi}{12}$. Thus, the integral is:

$$P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$$

steps.title: Find the antiderivative
steps.content:
To integrate $\sin(ax)$, we use the standard rule from the calculus section of the syllabus: $\int \sin(ax) dx = -\frac{1}{a}\cos(ax)$. 

In our case, $a = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$, so the antiderivative is:

$$\int \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$

Applying this to our definite integral:

$$\frac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_0^2$$

steps.title: Apply the limits
steps.content:
Now we apply the fundamental theorem of calculus by substituting the upper limit ($x=2$) and the lower limit ($x=0$):

$$\frac{\pi}{12} \left( \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi(2)}{6} \right) \right] - \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi(0)}{6} \right) \right] \right)$$

Simplifying the expressions inside the brackets:

$$\frac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{3} \right) + \frac{6}{\pi} \cos(0) \right]$$

steps.title: Evaluate and simplify
steps.content:
Recall the exact values for trigonometric functions: $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos(0) = 1$. 

Substitute these values into the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) &= \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) + \frac{6}{\pi} (1) \right] \\ &= \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\frac{3}{\pi} + \frac{6}{\pi} \right] \\ &= \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ \frac{3}{\pi} \right] \end{aligned}$$

Cancelling out $\pi$ and simplifying the fraction:

$$\frac{3}{12} = \frac{1}{4}$$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the probability $P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2)$, we integrate the probability density function $f(x)$ over the given interval $[0, 2]$. We use the constant $k = \frac{\pi}{12}$ determined from earlier parts of the problem.

$$P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$$

steps.title: Define the substitution
steps.content:
Using the **Integration by Substitution** approach, we let $u$ represent the inner function of the sine term to simplify the expression.

Let:
$$u = \frac{\pi x}{6}$$

Differentiating $u$ with respect to $x$ allows us to find $dx$:
$$\frac{du}{dx} = \frac{\pi}{6} \implies dx = \frac{6}{\pi} du$$

steps.title: Transform the limits
steps.content:
When performing a substitution in a definite integral, we must also update the limits from $x$-values to $u$-values.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Lower limit: } x &= 0 \implies u = \frac{\pi(0)}{6} = 0 \\ \text{Upper limit: } x &= 2 \implies u = \frac{\pi(2)}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Now, substitute these new values and the expression for $dx$ into the integral:

$$P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin(u) \cdot \left( \frac{6}{\pi} du \right)$$ 

The constants simplify: $\frac{\pi}{12} \cdot \frac{6}{\pi} = \frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Integrate and evaluate
steps.content:
We can now integrate with respect to $u$. The antiderivative of $\sin(u)$ is $-\cos(u)$.

$$\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sin(u) du = \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -\cos(u) \Big]_{0}^{\pi/3}$$

Substitute the limits:
$$\frac{1}{2} \left( -\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) - (-\cos(0)) \right)$$

Using known trigonometric values:
- $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = 0.5$
- $\cos(0) = 1$

$$\frac{1}{2} \left( -0.5 + 1 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

Thus, $P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \frac{1}{4}$.

steps.title: Identify the PDF
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** approach, we first look at the Probability Density Function (PDF) provided. In previous parts of this question, we determined that the normalization constant is $k = \frac{\pi}{12}$.

The function is defined as:

$$f(x) = \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$

for $0 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$.

steps.title: Set up the CDF integral
steps.content:
The Cumulative Distribution Function, $F(x)$, is the indefinite integral of the PDF. We use the rule for integrating $\sin(ax)$:

$$\int \sin(ax) \, dx = -\frac{1}{a}\cos(ax) + C$$

Setting up the integral for our function:

$$F(x) = \int \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$$

Applying the rule where $a = \frac{\pi}{6}$:

$$F(x) = \frac{\pi}{12} \left( -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right) + C$$

steps.title: Simplify and find C
steps.content:
First, let's simplify the coefficient:

$$\frac{\pi}{12} \times -\frac{6}{\pi} = -\frac{6}{12} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

So, $F(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) + C$.

We are given that $F(0) = 0$ (since the probability of $X < 0$ is zero). Let's solve for $C$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= -\frac{1}{2} \cos(0) + C \\ 0 &= -\frac{1}{2}(1) + C \\ C &= \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the general CDF is: $$F(x) = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$$, or $$F(x) = \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right)$$ for $0 \le x \le 6$.

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
To show that $P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = \frac{1}{4}$, we evaluate the CDF at the upper limit $x=2$:

$$P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = F(2) - F(0)$$

Since $F(0) = 0$, we just need to calculate $F(2)$:

$$F(2) = \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \cos \left( \frac{\pi(2)}{6} \right) \right)$$

$$F(2) = \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \cos \left( \frac{\pi}{3} \right) \right)$$

steps.title: Evaluate trig values
steps.content:
Recall that $\cos \left( \frac{\pi}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$ (or $0.5$). Substituting this into our expression:

$$\begin{aligned} F(2) &= \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the required result.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
We have shown that using the Cumulative Distribution Function $F(x) = \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right)$:

$$P(0 \leqslant X \leqslant 2) = F(2) = \frac{1}{4}$$

steps.title: Identify $Q_1$ and the median
steps.content:
From previous work in Part 5, we know that the first quartile is:

$$Q_1 = 2$$

To use the symmetry approach, we first identify the center of the distribution. The probability density function $f(x) = k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ is defined on the interval $[0, 6]$. The sine function reaches its maximum at the midpoint of its arch. In this case, the distribution is perfectly symmetric about the median:

$$\text{Median} = 3$$

steps.title: Apply symmetry to find $Q_3$
steps.content:
Because the PDF is symmetric about $x = 3$, the distance from the median to the first quartile ($Q_1$) must be equal to the distance from the median to the third quartile ($Q_3$).

We can calculate this distance ($d$) as:

$$d = \text{Median} - Q_1 = 3 - 2 = 1$$

Adding this same distance to the other side of the median gives us $Q_3$:

$$Q_3 = \text{Median} + d = 3 + 1 = 4$$

steps.title: Calculate the interquartile range
steps.content:
The interquartile range (IQR) is the difference between the third and first quartiles:

$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$$

Substituting our values:

$$\text{IQR} = 4 - 2 = 2$$

steps.title: Identify the task and $Q_1$
steps.content:
To find the interquartile range (IQR), we need the difference between the upper quartile ($Q_3$) and the lower quartile ($Q_1$). From our work in Part 5, we already know:

$$Q_1 = 2$$

We also know the value of the constant $k$ from previous parts is $\frac{\pi}{12}$. Now, we just need to find $Q_3$ to complete the calculation.

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
The upper quartile $Q_3$ is the value such that the area under the probability density function from the start (0) to $Q_3$ is $0.75$. 

We set up the integral equation using $f(x) = \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right)$:

$$\int_{0}^{Q_3} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = 0.75$$

steps.title: Perform the integration
steps.content:
Using the integration rule for sine functions, $\int \sin(ax) dx = -\frac{1}{a}\cos(ax)$, we integrate the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \left[ \frac{\pi}{12} \cdot \left( -\frac{6}{\pi} \right) \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{0}^{Q_3} &= 0.75 \\ \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{0}^{Q_3} &= 0.75 \end{aligned}$$

Now, we apply the limits:

$$-\frac{1}{2} \left( \cos \left( \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} \right) - \cos(0) \right) = 0.75$$

steps.title: Solve for $Q_3$
steps.content:
Since $\cos(0) = 1$, we can simplify and solve for $Q_3$:

$$\begin{aligned} -\frac{1}{2} \left( \cos \left( \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} \right) - 1 \right) &= 0.75 \\ \cos \left( \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} \right) - 1 &= -1.5 \\ \cos \left( \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} \right) &= -0.5 \end{aligned}$$

Taking the inverse cosine within our domain $[0, 6]$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} &= \arccos(-0.5) \\ \frac{\pi Q_3}{6} &= \frac{2\pi}{3} \\ Q_3 &= \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{6}{\pi} = 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the interquartile range using $Q_3 = 4$ and $Q_1 = 2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{IQR} &= Q_3 - Q_1 \\ \text{IQR} &= 4 - 2 \\ \text{IQR} &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result matches the expectations from the symmetry of the distribution.

- Calculate final answer: $= \dfrac{1}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall conditional probability formula
steps.content:
To calculate the conditional probability $P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3)$, we use the definition from the formula booklet:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

In this context:
- Event $A$ is $X \leq 4$
- Event $B$ is $X \geq 3$
- The intersection $A \cap B$ is the region where $3 \leq X \leq 4$.

So, we need to calculate:

$$P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3) = \frac{P(3 \leq X \leq 4)}{P(X \geq 3)}$$

steps.title: Find the constant k
steps.content:
Before calculating specific probabilities, we need the value of $k$. For a PDF, the total area under the curve must be 1:

$$\int_{0}^{6} k \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = 1$$

Integrating analytically:
$$\begin{aligned} k \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{0}^{6} &= 1 \\ -\frac{6k}{\pi} \left( \cos(\pi) - \cos(0) \right) &= 1 \\ -\frac{6k}{\pi} (-1 - 1) &= 1 \\ \frac{12k}{\pi} &= 1 \implies k = \frac{\pi}{12} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate denominator P(X ≥ 3)
steps.content:
Now we find the probability of the condition $P(X \geq 3)$ by integrating from 3 to 6:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 3) &= \int_{3}^{6} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx \\ &= \frac{\pi}{12} \left[ -\frac{6}{\pi} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{3}^{6} \\ &= -\frac{1}{2} \left( \cos(\pi) - \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \right) \\ &= -\frac{1}{2} (-1 - 0) = \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate numerator P(3 ≤ X ≤ 4)
steps.content:
Next, we find the probability of the intersection $P(3 \leq X \leq 4)$:

$$\begin{aligned} P(3 \leq X \leq 4) &= \int_{3}^{4} \frac{\pi}{12} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx \\ &= \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \cos \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) \right]_{3}^{4} \\ &= -\frac{1}{2} \left( \cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \right) \\ &= -\frac{1}{2} \left( -\frac{1}{2} - 0 \right) = \frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine final conditional probability
steps.content:
Substitute these values back into our conditional probability formula:

$$P(X \le 4 | X \ge 3) = \frac{1/4}{1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{2}{1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

According to the markscheme, correctly identifying the formula components earns **M1**, and the final calculation leads to **A1**.

steps.title: Define the conditional probability
steps.content:
To calculate the conditional probability $P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3)$, we use the definition of conditional probability from the formula booklet:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

In our context, the event $A$ is $X \leq 4$ and the event $B$ is $X \geq 3$. The intersection $A \cap B$ is the range where both conditions are true: $3 \leq X \leq 4$.

So, we need to calculate:

$$P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3) = \frac{P(3 \leq X \leq 4)}{P(X \geq 3)}$$

steps.title: Find the constant k
steps.content:
First, we must find the value of $k$ such that the total area under the probability density function (PDF) is equal to 1. This means:

$$\int_{0}^{6} k \sin \left( \dfrac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx = 1$$

Using the numerical solver on your GDC, we can find the value of $k$.

gdc.instructions: In the Equation Solver or by graphing, find K such that ∫(K*sin(πx/6), x, 0, 6) = 1.

ti84.instructions: Go to [MATH] [B:Solver] (or [0:Numeric Solver]). Enter the equation 0 = fnInt(K*sin(π*X/6), X, 0, 6) - 1. Set an initial guess for K and solve.

desmos.instructions: Type the integral and solve for k: ∫_{0}^{6} k sin(πx/6) dx = 1.

calculator.description: Find k such that the integral from 0 to 6 is 1.

steps.title: Calculate probability areas numerically
steps.content:
From the solver, we find $k \approx 0.2618$ (specifically $k = \frac{\pi}{12}$). Now, we use the numerical integration function on the GDC to find the two specific probabilities needed for our formula:

1.  **Numerator:** $P(3 \leq X \leq 4) = \int_{3}^{4} 0.2618 \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$
2.  **Denominator:** $P(X \geq 3) = \int_{3}^{6} 0.2618 \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right) dx$

gdc.instructions: Use the integral function: ∫(0.2618*sin(πx/6), x, 3, 4) and ∫(0.2618*sin(πx/6), x, 3, 6).

ti84.instructions:
Use [MATH] [9:fnInt]. 
For P(3 < X < 4): fnInt(0.2618*sin(π*X/6), X, 3, 4) ≈ 0.25
For P(X > 3): fnInt(0.2618*sin(π*X/6), X, 3, 6) ≈ 0.5

desmos.instructions: Calculate the two integrals: ∫_{3}^{4} 0.2618 sin(πx/6) dx and ∫_{3}^{6} 0.2618 sin(πx/6) dx.

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical integrals for the numerator and denominator.

steps.title: Solve for the ratio
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the values we found into the conditional probability formula:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X \le 4 | X \ge 3) &= \frac{P(3 \le X \le 4)}{P(X \ge 3)} \\ &= \frac{0.25}{0.5} \\ &= 0.5 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result from the markscheme: the required probability is $\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Recall conditional probability formula
steps.content:
To calculate a conditional probability, we use the formula from the formula booklet:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

In our case, we want to find $P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3)$. This translates to:

$$P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3) = \frac{P(3 \leq X \leq 4)}{P(X \geq 3)}$$

steps.title: Use symmetry for P(X ≥ 3)
steps.content:
The PDF $f(x) = k \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{6} \right)$ is defined on the interval $[0, 6]$. Notice that this is exactly one half-cycle (one arch) of a sine wave, because $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\sin(\pi) = 0$.

A sine arch is perfectly symmetric about its peak. The peak occurs at the midpoint of the interval:

$$\text{Midpoint} = \frac{0 + 6}{2} = 3$$

Because the total area under any PDF must be $1$ and the graph is symmetric about $x = 3$, exactly half of the area lies to the right of $x = 3$. Therefore:

$$P(X \geq 3) = 0.5$$

steps.title: Substitute known area values
steps.content:
From the markscheme or previous calculations, we know the area between $x=3$ and $x=4$ is:

$$P(3 \leq X \leq 4) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

Now we can substitute our two values into the conditional probability formula:

$$P(X \leq 4 \mid X \geq 3) = \frac{0.25}{0.5}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Dividing $0.25$ by $0.5$ is equivalent to finding how many halves fit into a quarter:

$$\frac{0.25}{0.5} = 0.5$$

So, the final probability is $0.5$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.25 / 0.5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.25 / 0.5 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.25 / 0.5 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the division of the two probabilities.

steps.title: Identify the symmetry property
steps.content:
A key feature of the normal distribution is that the curve is perfectly symmetric about the mean, $\mu$. This means that the area under the curve to the left of the mean is a mirror image of the area to the right of the mean.

This property implies that for any constant $a$:
$$P(X < \mu - a) = P(X > \mu + a)$$ 

This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**.

highlights.text: follows a normal distribution with mean μ

steps.title: Apply symmetry to the values
steps.content:
In this problem, we are given the probability for the lower tail at a distance of $5$ units from the mean:
$$P(X < \mu - 5) = 0.2$$

Because of the symmetry of the bell curve, the probability of $X$ being more than $5$ units **above** the mean must be exactly equal to the probability of $X$ being more than $5$ units **below** the mean.

Therefore:
$$P(X > \mu + 5) = P(X < \mu - 5)$$

steps.title: State final probability
steps.content:
Substituting the known value into our symmetry equation:
$$P(X > \mu + 5) = 0.2$$

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark (**M1**) for indicating the use of symmetry (perhaps by drawing a sketch of the normal curve) and one mark (**A1**) for the correct final value.

steps.title: Analyze the given information
steps.content:
We are given that $X$ is a normally distributed random variable with mean $\mu$. We also know the probability for a specific region below the mean:

$$P(X < \mu - 5) = 0.2$$

This tells us that the area under the normal curve to the left of 5 units below the mean is $0.2$. This concept is covered in **Topic 4.9: Normal Distribution**.

steps.title: Standardize the given probability
steps.content:
To compare different normal distributions, we transform $X$ into the standard normal variable $Z$ using the formula $Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$.

For the value $X = \mu - 5$:

$$z_1 = \frac{(\mu - 5) - \mu}{\sigma} = -\frac{5}{\sigma}$$

So, the given information can be rewritten in terms of $Z$:

$$P\left(Z < -\frac{5}{\sigma}\right) = 0.2$$

steps.title: Standardize the target probability
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the probability we need to find: $P(X > \mu + 5)$. 

Standardizing the value $X = \mu + 5$:

$$z_2 = \frac{(\mu + 5) - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{5}{\sigma}$$

Our goal is to find $P\left(Z > \frac{5}{\sigma}\right)$.

steps.title: Apply the symmetry property
steps.content:
The Standard Normal Distribution is perfectly symmetrical about the mean ($Z = 0$). A key property of symmetry is:

$$P(Z < -z) = P(Z > z)$$

Comparing our standardized values:
- We know $P(Z < -\frac{5}{\sigma}) = 0.2$
- We want to find $P(Z > \frac{5}{\sigma})$

By the symmetry property, these two probabilities must be equal.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Since the regions are equidistant from the mean on opposite sides, their areas are identical:

$$P(X > \mu + 5) = 0.2$$

In an IB exam, showing a quick sketch of the normal curve with these two symmetric areas shaded would earn you the method mark (**M1**).

parts.content: $P(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5)$.
parts.markscheme:
**EITHER**
- $P(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5) = \frac{P(X > \mu - 5 \cap X < \mu + 5)}{P(X > \mu - 5)}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $= \frac{P(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5)}{P(X > \mu - 5)}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $= \frac{0.6}{0.8}$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

<note>A1 for denominator is independent of the previous A marks.</note>

**OR**
- Use of diagram <marks>M1</marks>

<note>Only award M1 if the region $\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5$ is indicated and used.</note>

- $P(X > \mu - 5) = 0.8$, $P(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5) = 0.6$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Probabilities can be shown on the diagram.</note>

- $= \frac{0.6}{0.8}$ <marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

**THEN**
- $= \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Apply conditional probability formula
steps.content:
To solve this, we start with the formula for conditional probability found in your data booklet:

$$\mathrm{P}(A|B) = \frac{\mathrm{P}(A \cap B)}{\mathrm{P}(B)}$$

In our case, let:
- $A$ be the event $X < \mu + 5$
- $B$ be the event $X > \mu - 5$

So we are looking for:
$$\mathrm{P}(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5) = \frac{\mathrm{P}(X < \mu + 5 \cap X > \mu - 5)}{\mathrm{P}(X > \mu - 5)}$$

steps.title: Simplify the intersection
steps.content:
The numerator represents the intersection of $X < \mu + 5$ and $X > \mu - 5$. This is simply the probability that $X$ falls between these two values:

$$\mathrm{P}(X < \mu + 5 \cap X > \mu - 5) = \mathrm{P}(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5)$$ 

This captures the middle region of the normal distribution curve centered around the mean $\mu$.

steps.title: Calculate the denominator
steps.content:
We are given that $\mathrm{P}(X < \mu - 5) = 0.2$. Since the total area under the normal curve is $1$, we can find the probability of the complement:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(X > \mu - 5) &= 1 - \mathrm{P}(X < \mu - 5) \\ &= 1 - 0.2 \\ &= 0.8 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us our denominator for the final fraction.

steps.title: Determine numerator using symmetry
steps.content:
The normal distribution is perfectly symmetrical about the mean $\mu$. 

Since $\mu - 5$ and $\mu + 5$ are the same distance from the mean, the 'tails' have equal area:
- Left tail: $\mathrm{P}(X < \mu - 5) = 0.2$
- Right tail: $\mathrm{P}(X > \mu + 5) = 0.2$

The area in the middle is the total area minus both tails:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5) &= 1 - [\mathrm{P}(X < \mu - 5) + \mathrm{P}(X > \mu + 5)] \\ &= 1 - [0.2 + 0.2] \\ &= 0.6 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Compute final probability
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our calculated values back into the conditional probability formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5) &= \frac{0.6}{0.8} \\ &= \frac{6}{8} \\ &= 0.75 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.6 ÷ 0.8 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.6 / 0.8 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.6 / 0.8 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final probability quotient.

steps.title: Define the conditional probability
steps.content:
To find $P(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5)$, we first use the definition of conditional probability from the data booklet:

$$\mathrm{P}(A|B)=\frac{\mathrm{P}(A\cap B)}{\mathrm{P}(B)}$$

In our case, this becomes:

$$P(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5) = \frac{P(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5)}{P(X > \mu - 5)}$$

steps.title: Visualize symmetry and tails
steps.content:
A normal distribution is perfectly symmetric around its mean, $\mu$. 

We are given that the area in the left tail is $P(X < \mu - 5) = 0.2$. Because the curve is symmetric, the area in the right tail must be equal:

$$P(X > \mu + 5) = 0.2$$

steps.title: Calculate the denominator
steps.content:
The denominator for our conditional probability is $P(X > \mu - 5)$. 

Since the total area under the normal curve is $1$, and the area to the left of $\mu - 5$ is $0.2$, the area to the right is:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X > \mu - 5) &= 1 - P(X < \mu - 5) \\ &= 1 - 0.2 \\ &= 0.8 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 - 0.2 on the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 - 0.2 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 1 - 0.2.

calculator.description: Verify the subtraction for the denominator.

steps.title: Calculate the numerator
steps.content:
The numerator is $P(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5)$, which represents the "middle" region of the graph.

Graphically, we can find this by taking the total area ($1$) and subtracting the two symmetric tails we identified ($0.2$ on each side):

$$\begin{aligned} P(\mu - 5 < X < \mu + 5) &= 1 - P(X < \mu - 5) - P(X > \mu + 5) \\ &= 1 - 0.2 - 0.2 \\ &= 0.6 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 - 0.2 - 0.2 on the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 - 0.2 - 0.2 on the main screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the middle area by subtracting both tails.

steps.title: Solve the final ratio
steps.content:
Now substitute the values back into the conditional probability formula:

$$\begin{aligned} P(X < \mu + 5 \mid X > \mu - 5) &= \frac{0.6}{0.8} \\ &= \frac{6}{8} \\ &= 0.75 \end{aligned}$$

This represents the proportion of the area to the right of $\mu - 5$ that also falls below $\mu + 5$.

steps.title: Define the probability
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that a packet is underweight. Since the weight $X$ follows a normal distribution and a packet is considered underweight if it weighs less than $250$ grams, we need to find:

$$P(X < 250)$$ 

This corresponds to the area under the normal curve to the left of $250$.

highlights.text: underweight if it weighs less than 250 grams

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
From the problem, we are given the mean $(\mu)$ and the standard deviation $(\sigma)$ for the normal distribution:

*   $$\mu = 253$$
*   $$\sigma = 1.5$$

To find the probability $P(X < 250)$, we will use the **Normal Cumulative Distribution Function (normCdf)** on our GDC. For this calculation, we define the boundaries:
*   **Lower Bound:** $-\infty$ (usually entered as $-10^{99}$ or $-1E99$ on a calculator)
*   **Upper Bound:** $250$

The calculator gives a value of approximately $0.022750...$

$$P(X < 250) = 0.0228$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for the final probability.

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content:
The question states that the weights $X$ follow a normal distribution. We are given the following parameters:

*   Mean $(\mu) = 253$
*   Standard deviation $(\sigma) = 1.5$

A packet is considered underweight if $X < 250$. Our goal is to find the probability $P(X < 250)$. This is covered in **Topic 4: Normal Distribution**.

steps.title: Calculate the Z-score
steps.content:
To standardize the value, we use the Z-score formula. This formula tells us how many standard deviations the value $x = 250$ is away from the mean:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:

$$z = \frac{250 - 253}{1.5} = \frac{-3}{1.5} = -2$$

steps.title: Find the standardized probability
steps.content:
Now we need to find the probability that a value in the standard normal distribution $Z \sim \text{N}(0, 1)$ is less than $-2$. 

Mathematically, we look for $P(Z < -2)$. Using a GDC or a standard normal table, we find this area.

steps.title: Identify parameters and goal
steps.content:
We are told that the weight of cookie packets follows a normal distribution $X \sim \text{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. From the previous part of the question, we know that the standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.5$. 

The goal is to find a new mean, $\mu$, such that the probability of a packet being underweight (less than $250$ grams) is exactly $0.002$.

$$P(X < 250) = 0.002$$

highlights.text: probability that a packet is underweight should be 0.002
underweight if it weighs less than 250 grams

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Since we are given a probability and need to find a value, we use the **Inverse Normal** function on a calculator. We want to find the $z$-score such that the area to the left is $0.002$ in a standard normal distribution $Z \sim \text{N}(0, 1)$.

Using technology, we find:
$$z \approx -2.87816...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Normal, and Inverse Normal. Set the Area (or Central Probability) to 0.002, μ to 0, and σ to 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.002, μ: 0, σ: 1, and Paste.

desmos.instructions: Type `inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.002)` into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the z-score for a left-tail probability of 0.002.

steps.title: Apply the standardization formula
steps.content:
The relationship between a specific value $x$, the mean $\mu$, the standard deviation $\sigma$, and the $z$-score is given by the standardization formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our known values ($z = -2.87816$, $x = 250$, and $\sigma = 1.5$):

$$-2.87816 = \frac{250 - \mu}{1.5}$$

steps.title: Solve for the mean
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for the new mean $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} -2.87816 \times 1.5 &= 250 - \mu \\ -4.31724 &= 250 - \mu \\ \mu &= 250 + 4.31724 \\ \mu &= 254.31724... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two decimal places as requested, we get:
$$\mu = 254.32 \text{ g}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 250 - (1.5 * -2.87816) on the main screen.

steps.title: Identify the target probability
steps.content:
The question states that a packet is underweight if its weight $X$ is less than $250$ grams. We want the probability of this occurring to be $0.002$. 

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$P(X < 250) = 0.002$$

We know from the previous part of the problem that the standard deviation remains unchanged at $\sigma = 1.5$. We need to find the new value of the mean $\mu$.

highlights.text: probability that a packet is underweight should be 0.002

steps.title: Set up the GDC Solver
steps.content:
To solve for $\mu$ directly, we can use the **Numerical Solver** (or Equation Solver) feature on your GDC. We will use the Normal Cumulative Distribution Function (often called `normCDF` or `NormCD`) which calculates the area under the normal curve.

The equation to solve is:
$$\text{normCDF}(-\infty, 250, \mu, 1.5) = 0.002$$

In the solver, we rewrite this so that one side equals zero:
$$\text{normCDF}(-10^{99}, 250, x, 1.5) - 0.002 = 0$$
where $x$ represents the unknown mean $\mu$.

steps.title: Set up standardization formula

We use the standardization formula to relate the $z$-score to our specific distribution values. The formula is:

Substituting our known values:

$$-2.87816... = \frac{250 - 253}{\sigma}$$

This step is crucial and earns the method mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $\sigma$:

$$\begin{aligned} -2.87816... &= \frac{-3}{\sigma} \\ \sigma &= \frac{-3}{-2.87816...} \\ \sigma &\approx 1.04233... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:
$$\sigma = 1.04$$

gdc.instructions: Divide -3 by the z-score value obtained previously.

ti84.instructions: Perform the division using the saved Ans from the previous step.

desmos.instructions: Divide -3 by the calculated z-score.

calculator.description: Calculate the final value of sigma.

steps.title: Identify the parameters
steps.content:
We need to find the new standard deviation, $\sigma$, given the following conditions from the problem:

*   The mean weight is returned to $\mu = 253$ grams.
*   The weight limit for being underweight is $x = 250$ grams.
*   The probability of a packet being underweight is $P(X < 250) = 0.002$.

Since weights follow a normal distribution, we can model this as $X \sim N(253, \sigma^2)$ and solve for the unknown $\sigma$ using the cumulative distribution function (CDF).

highlights.text: probability that a packet is underweight should be 0.002

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Using our Graphing Display Calculator (GDC), we know that the probability $P(X < 250)$ is calculated using the `normCDF` function. Our goal is to solve the following equation for $\sigma$:

$$\text{normCDF}(-\infty, 250, 253, \sigma) = 0.002$$

In the solver, we will replace $\sigma$ with a variable (like $x$) and set the lower bound to a very small number (like $-10^{99}$) to represent negative infinity.

steps.title: Solve using GDC Numerical Solver
steps.content: Now, we use the numerical solver function on the GDC to find the value of $\sigma$ directly without manual algebraic manipulation.

gdc.instructions:
1. Use the 'nSolve' function from the Math menu.
2. Type: nSolve(normCDF(-1E99, 250, 253, x) = 0.002, x).
3. Press [ENTER].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] and select [B:Solver...].
2. Enter the equation: 0 = normCDF(-1E99, 250, 253, X) - 0.002.
3. Provide a guess for X (e.g., X=1) and press [ALPHA][SOLVE].

desmos.instructions:
1. In the first line, type: f(x) = normaldist(253, x).cdf(250).
2. In the second line, type: y = 0.002.
3. Find the intersection point where x > 0.

calculator.description: Use the Numerical Solver to find the value of sigma.

steps.title: State the final value
steps.content:
From the calculator, we find that:

$$\sigma \approx 1.04233...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, as is standard in IB exams, we get:

$$\sigma = 1.04$$

This matches the result required by the markscheme, where a z-score method would yield the same value.

questions.specification:
A biometric security system generates match scores for facial recognition. The scores for authorized employees are normally distributed with a mean of 82 and a standard deviation of 4. The scores for unauthorized individuals are normally distributed with a mean of 68 and a standard deviation of 9. The security system is programmed with the null hypothesis that any person attempting access is an authorized employee. 

Access is denied and the user is flagged as 'unauthorized' if their match score is below 75. On a typical day, 92% of all access attempts are made by authorized employees.

steps.title: Identify given probabilities
steps.content:
We are told that the battery life $X$ follows a normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. From the question, we can write two probability statements:

- $P(X < 16) = 0.56$
- $P(X < 17) = 0.94$

To solve for the two unknowns, $\mu$ and $\sigma$, we will use the **Algebraic Z-score Method**. This involves finding the corresponding $z$-scores for these probabilities from the standard normal distribution $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

If you need a refresher on how the normal distribution works, check out **Topic 4.9: Normal Distribution**.

highlights.text: 56% of Infiglow batteries have a life of less than 16 hours
94% have a life less than 17 hours

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
Using the inverse normal function on your GDC, find the $z$-scores that correspond to the cumulative areas of $0.56$ and $0.94$.

For $P(Z < z_1) = 0.56$:
$$z_1 \approx 0.150968...$$

For $P(Z < z_2) = 0.94$:
$$z_2 \approx 1.55477...$$

These values represent how many standard deviations the values $16$ and $17$ are away from the mean $\mu$.

gdc.instructions: Go to Statistics -> Distributions -> Normal -> Inverse Normal. Set Area to 0.56, then 0.94, with μ=0 and σ=1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 3:invNorm(. Enter Area: 0.56, μ: 0, σ: 1. Repeat for Area: 0.94.

desmos.instructions: Type `inverseNormal(normaldist(0,1), 0.56)` and `inverseNormal(normaldist(0,1), 0.94)`.

calculator.description: Calculate inverse normal values for areas 0.56 and 0.94.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We use the $z$-score formula, which relates the observed value $x$, the mean $\mu$, and the standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values into this formula gives us two equations:

1. For $x = 16$: $16 - \mu = 0.150968\sigma$
2. For $x = 17$: $17 - \mu = 1.55477\sigma$

steps.title: Solve for sigma
steps.content:
To eliminate $\mu$, subtract the first equation from the second:

$$\begin{aligned} (17 - \mu) - (16 - \mu) &= 1.55477\sigma - 0.150968\sigma \\ 1 &= 1.403802\sigma \end{aligned}$$

Now, solve for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{1}{1.403802} \approx 0.712351...$$

To three significant figures, **$\sigma = 0.712$**.

steps.title: Solve for mu
steps.content:
Substitute the value of $\sigma$ back into one of the original equations (using the unrounded value for accuracy):

$$\begin{aligned} 16 - \mu &= 0.150968(0.712351...) \\ 16 - \mu &= 0.107542... \\ \mu &= 16 - 0.107542... \\ \mu &\approx 15.8924... \end{aligned}$$

To three significant figures, **$\mu = 15.9$**.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
Rounding both values to the standard 3 significant figures required by the IB:

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 15.9 \\ \sigma &= 0.712 \end{aligned}$$

In an exam, you earn marks for stating the correct $z$-scores, setting up the two linear equations, and showing an attempt to solve them simultaneously.

For the second condition:
$$P(X < 17) = 0.94$$

steps.title: Set up linear equations
steps.content:
Next, we use the $z$-score formula, $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, to set up two equations. Since we want to use a graphical approach, we will rearrange both equations to solve for $\mu$ in terms of $\sigma$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Equation 1: } \mu &= 16 - 0.150968\sigma \\ \text{Equation 2: } \mu &= 17 - 1.55477\sigma \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Graph and find intersection
steps.content:
Now, treat these as linear functions where $y = \mu$ and $x = \sigma$. We will graph both lines and find their point of intersection $(x, y)$, which corresponds to $(\sigma, \mu)$.

Graph the following:
$$y_1 = 16 - 0.150968x$$
$$y_2 = 17 - 1.55477x$$

The intersection point provides our values for $\sigma$ and $\mu$.

$$\begin{aligned} \mu &= 15.9 \text{ hours} \\ \sigma &= 0.712 \text{ hours} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Formulate linear equations
steps.content:
We use the $z$-score formula from the data booklet to relate the values to $\mu$ and $\sigma$:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} \Rightarrow x = \mu + z\sigma$$

Substituting our values gives a system of two linear equations:
1. $16 = \mu + 0.150968\sigma$
2. $17 = \mu + 1.55477\sigma$

Rearranging into the standard form ($A\mu + B\sigma = C$) for the GDC solver:
$$\begin{aligned} 1\mu + 0.150968\sigma &= 16 \\ 1\mu + 1.55477\sigma &= 17 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
Now, we input the coefficients into the Simultaneous Equation Solver on the GDC to find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ directly.

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.150968 & 16 \\ 1 & 1.55477 & 17 \end{bmatrix}$$
