steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
The battery life $X$ follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, written as $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. From the question, we are given two specific probabilities:

1. 56% have a life of less than 16 hours: $P(X < 16) = 0.56$
2. 94% have a life less than 17 hours: $P(X < 17) = 0.94$

We need to find $P(X \ge 15)$. To do this, we must first find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ using standardization.

steps.title: Find the z-scores
steps.content:
To standardize these values, we find the $z$-scores that correspond to the 56th and 94th percentiles using the inverse normal function on your GDC.

For $P(Z < z_1) = 0.56$, we find $z_1 \approx 0.150968$.
For $P(Z < z_2) = 0.94$, we find $z_2 \approx 1.554774$.

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We use the standardization formula to create a system of linear equations:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:
1. $0.150968 = \frac{16 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 0.150968\sigma = 16 - \mu$
2. $1.554774 = \frac{17 - \mu}{\sigma} \implies 1.554774\sigma = 17 - \mu$

steps.title: Solve for mean and SD
steps.content:
Subtract the first equation from the second to eliminate $\mu$:

$$\begin{aligned} (1.554774 - 0.150968)\sigma &= 17 - 16 \\ 1.403806\sigma &= 1 \\ \sigma &\approx 0.712351 \end{aligned}$$

Now substitute $\sigma$ back into the first equation to find $\mu$:

$$\mu = 16 - 0.150968(0.712351) \approx 15.8925$$

steps.content:
Now that we have $\mu \approx 15.8925$ and $\sigma \approx 0.712351$, we can find the probability that a battery lasts at least 15 hours:

$$P(X \ge 15)$$

Using the Normal CDF function on your GDC with lower bound 15, upper bound $\infty$ (or a very large number like $10^{99}$), $\mu = 15.8925$, and $\sigma = 0.712351$:

parts.content: Find the probability that the time taken for a randomly chosen customer to be dealt with by an employee is greater than 180 seconds.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters

First, we identify that the time taken, $X$, follows a normal distribution. From the question, we are given the mean ($\mu$) and the standard deviation ($\sigma$).

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content:
First, we define the random variable $X$ as the time taken for an employee to deal with a customer. From the problem, we know $X$ follows a normal distribution with a mean ($\mu$) and a standard deviation ($\sigma$).

$$X \sim N(150, 45^2)$$

steps.title: Calculate the Z-score
steps.content:
To standardize the variable, we calculate the Z-score for $x = 180$ seconds. This tells us how many standard deviations $180$ is away from the mean.

The formula for the Z-score is:
$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} z &= \frac{180 - 150}{45} \\ z &= \frac{30}{45} \\ z &\approx 0.667 \end{aligned}$$

$$
P(Z > 0.666 \dots)
$$

Using a standard normal distribution curve ($Z \sim N(0, 1)$), we can find this area using a calculator.

parts.content: Find the probability that the time taken by an employee to deal with a queue of three customers is less than nine minutes.
parts.markscheme:
Note: In this question, accept answers that round correctly to 2 significant figures.

  • Required to find $P\left( {{X_1} + {X_2} + {X_3} < 540} \right)$
  • Let $S = {X_1} + {X_2} + {X_3}$
  • $E\left( S \right) = 450$ A1
  • $Var\left( S \right) = 3\,Var\left( X \right)$ M1
  • $= 3 \times {45^2}\,\left( { \Rightarrow \sigma = 45\sqrt 3 } \right)\,\left( { = 6075} \right)$ A1
  • $P\left( {S < 540} \right) = 0.876$ A1

4 marks total

Note: In Parts 2 and 3 condone incorrect notation, eg, $3X$ for ${X_1} + {X_2} + {X_3}$.

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
To compare the total time with the given mean and standard deviation, we first need to convert the limit from minutes to seconds.

Since there are 60 seconds in a minute:
$$9 \text{ minutes} = 9 \times 60 = 540 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Calculate the total mean
steps.content:
Let $X$ be the time taken for one customer, where $X \sim N(150, 45^2)$. For a queue of three customers, we define a new random variable $S$ as the sum of three independent times:
$$S = X_1 + X_2 + X_3$$

The expected value (mean) of the sum is the sum of the individual means:
$$\text{E}(S) = 150 + 150 + 150 = 450 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.content:
Since the times are independent, the variance of the sum is the sum of the variances:

$$
\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(S) &= \text{Var}(X_{1}) + \text{Var}(X_{2}) + \text{Var}(X_{3}) \\ \text{Var}(S) &= 45^2 + 45^2 + 45^2 = 3 \times 45^2 = 6075 \end{aligned}
$$

To use this in our calculator later, we find the standard deviation ($\sigma_S$):

$$
\sigma_S = \sqrt{6075} \approx 77.94 \text{ seconds}
$$

steps.title: Convert time to seconds
steps.content:
The time for a single customer is given in seconds, but the total limit is given in minutes. We must convert the total limit to seconds so our units are consistent.

$$9 \text{ minutes} = 9 \times 60 = 540 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Calculate the average limit
steps.content:
Using the **Distribution of Sample Mean** approach, we want to find the probability that the *average* time taken for $n = 3$ customers is within the limit. 

If the total time for 3 customers must be less than 540 seconds, then the average time per customer (the sample mean, $\bar{x}$) must be:

$$\bar{x} < \frac{540}{3} = 180 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Define sample mean distribution
steps.content:
The distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ for a sample of size $n$ from a normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is given by:

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

Given $\mu = 150$, $\sigma = 45$, and $n = 3$:
- The mean of $\bar{X}$ is $\mu_{\bar{x}} = 150$
- The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{45}{\sqrt{3}}$

- Lower bound: $0$ (or $-\infty$)
- Upper bound: $180$
- Mean $(\mu)$: $150$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{45}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 25.98 \dots$

$$
P(\bar{X} < 180) \approx 0.876
$$

steps.title: Convert units to seconds
steps.content:
First, we need to ensure all our units match. The mean and standard deviation are given in seconds, but the question asks for the probability regarding a time less than nine minutes. 

$$9 \text{ minutes} = 9 \times 60 = 540 \text{ seconds}$$

steps.title: Define the sum distribution
steps.content:
Let $X$ be the time taken for one customer, where $X \sim N(150, 45^2)$. We are dealing with a queue of three independent customers, so we define a new random variable $S$ as the sum of these three times:

$$
S = X_{1} + X_{2} + X_{3}
$$

To find the probability $P(S < 540)$, we first need the mean and variance of $S$.

highlights.text: mean 150 seconds and standard deviation 45 seconds

steps.title: Calculate mean and variance
steps.content:
For independent random variables, the mean and variance of the sum are the sums of the individual means and variances:

$$
\begin{aligned} E(S) &= 150 + 150 + 150 = 450 \\ \text{Var}(S) &= 45^2 + 45^2 + 45^2 = 3 \times 45^2 = 6075 \end{aligned}
$$

To find the standard deviation ($\sigma_S$), we take the square root of the variance:

$$
\sigma_S = \sqrt{6075} = 45\sqrt{3} \approx 77.94 \text{ seconds}
$$

steps.title: Determine the Z-score
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the Z-score for the boundary value of $540$ seconds. The Z-score tells us how many standard deviations our value is from the mean.

$$
Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Substituting our values for the sum distribution:

$$
Z = \frac{540 - 450}{77.942 \dots} = \frac{90}{77.942 \dots} \approx 1.1547
$$

steps.title: Find the final probability
steps.content:
We are looking for $P(S < 540)$, which is equivalent to $P(Z < 1.1547)$ on the standard normal curve. 

Using a calculator or standard normal tables, we find:

$$
P(Z < 1.1547) = 0.875893 \dots
$$

Rounding to three significant figures, the probability is $0.876$.

parts.content:
At the start of the day, one employee, Amanda, has a queue of four customers. A second employee, Brian, has a queue of three customers. You may assume they work independently.

Find the probability that Amanda’s queue will be dealt with before Brian’s queue.
parts.markscheme:
Note: In this question, accept answers that round correctly to 2 significant figures.

  • Let $Y = \left( {{X_1} + {X_2} + {X_3} + {X_4}} \right) - \left( {{X_5} + {X_6} + {X_7}} \right)$ M1
  • $E\left( Y \right) = E\left( X \right) = 150$ A1
  • $Var\left( Y \right) = 4\,Var\left( X \right) + 3\,Var\left( X \right)\left( { = 7\,Var\left( X \right)} \right)$ M1
  • $= 14175$ A1
  • Required to find $P\left( {Y < 0} \right)$ M1
  • $= 0.104$ A1

6 marks total

steps.title: Define the linear combination
steps.content:
To find the probability that Amanda finishes before Brian, we need to compare the total time taken for Amanda's 4 customers to the total time for Brian's 3 customers. 

Let $X$ represent the time to deal with a single customer, where $X \sim N(150, 45^2)$. 
- Amanda's total time: $A = X_{1} + X_{2} + X_{3} + X_{4}$
- Brian's total time: $B = X_{5} + X_{6} + X_{7}$

We want to find $P(A < B)$, which is the same as $P(A - B < 0)$. Let's define a new normal random variable $Y$ for the difference:

$$
Y = (X_{1} + X_{2} + X_{3} + X_{4}) - (X_{5} + X_{6} + X_{7})
$$

This approach uses concepts from **Topic 4.14: Linear transformation of a single RV** and **4.15: Central limit theorem** (specifically linear combinations of normal variables).

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
The mean of a sum (or difference) of independent random variables is simply the sum (or difference) of their means.

$$
\begin{aligned} E(Y) &= E(X_{1} + X_{2} + X_{3} + X_{4}) - E(X_{5} + X_{6} + X_{7}) \\ E(Y) &= 4 \times 150 - 3 \times 150 \\ E(Y) &= 150 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Calculate the variance
steps.content:
For independent variables, variances always **add**, even when we are subtracting the variables themselves. This is because the uncertainty (spread) of the total difference increases with every additional random variable included.

The variance of $Y$ is the sum of the variances of all 7 independent customer service times:

$$
\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(Y) &= \text{Var}(X_{1}) + \text{Var}(X_{2}) + \text{Var}(X_{3}) + \text{Var}(X_{4}) + \text{Var}(X_{5}) + \text{Var}(X_{6}) + \text{Var}(X_{7}) \\ \text{Var}(Y) &= 7 \times \text{Var}(X) \\ \text{Var}(Y) &= 7 \times 45^2 \\ \text{Var}(Y) &= 14175 \end{aligned}
$$

So, the combined variable follows a normal distribution: $Y \sim N(150, 14175)$.

steps.title: Find the probability
steps.content:
Now we need to calculate $P(Y < 0)$ using the distribution $Y \sim N(150, 14175)$. We use the Normal Cumulative Distribution function on our calculator.

$$
\begin{aligned} P(Y < 0) &\approx 0.103856 \dots \\ &\approx 0.104 \text{ (to 3 s.f.)} \end{aligned}
$$

parts.content: Sam and Alex each run one trial. Find the probability that Sam's time is at least $0.5$ seconds faster than Alex's time.
parts.markscheme:
Let $S$ be Sam's time and $A$ be Alex's time.
$S \sim \text{N}(11.2, 0.15^2)$ and $A \sim \text{N}(11.5, 0.20^2)$
Let $D = A - S$ (or $S - A$)
$\text{E}(D) = 11.5 - 11.2 = 0.3$ <marks>A1</marks>
$\text{Var}(D) = \text{Var}(A) + \text{Var}(S) = 0.20^2 + 0.15^2 = 0.0625$ <marks>M1A1</marks>
$D \sim \text{N}(0.3, 0.25^2)$
$\text{P}(D \geq 0.5) = 0.212$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept $0.211855...$

questions.specification:
A manufacturer produces 1500 boxes of breakfast cereal every day.

The weights of these boxes are normally distributed with a mean of 502 grams and a standard deviation of 2 grams.

All boxes of cereal with a weight between 497.5 grams and 505 grams are sold. The manufacturer’s income from the sale of each box of cereal is $2.00.

The manufacturer recycles any box of cereal with a weight not between 497.5 grams and 505 grams. The manufacturer’s recycling cost is $0.16 per box.

A different manufacturer produces boxes of cereal with weights that are normally distributed with a mean of 350 grams and a standard deviation of 1.8 grams.

This manufacturer sells all boxes of cereal that are above a minimum weight, $w$.

They sell 97% of the cereal boxes produced.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Finding the probability values of normally distributed variables
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) require calculating probabilities for a normally distributed weight falling inside/outside given bounds, then using these probabilities to find expected recycling cost and income.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Finding the value of X using inverse normal
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for the minimum weight w such that 97% of boxes are sold, i.e., finding a cutoff value from a normal distribution via an inverse normal/quantile.

parts.content: Calculate the manufacturer’s expected daily recycling cost.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate the expected recycling cost: $1500 \times 0.16 \times 0.079031\ldots$ M1
    • Award A1 for $1500 \times 0.16 \times \text{their }(1 - 0.920968\ldots)$.
  • OR
  • Calculate using the number of recycled boxes: $(1500 - 1381.45) \times 0.16$ M1
    • Award M1 for $(1500 - \text{their } 1381.45) \times 0.16$.
  • $= \text{\$}19.0 \, (18.9676\ldots)$ A1 FT G2

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the distribution
steps.content:
First, we identify the parameters for the normal distribution of the cereal box weights ($W$). The problem provides the mean ($\mu$) and the standard deviation ($\sigma$).

$$W \sim N(502, 2^2)$$

This distribution helps us calculate the probability of any weight range occurring.

steps.title: Calculate probability of selling
steps.content:
We are told that a box is sold if its weight is between $497.5\text{ g}$ and $505\text{ g}$. We can use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to find this probability:

$$P(\text{Sold}) = P(497.5 \leq W \leq 505)$$

Using the normal cumulative distribution function (Ncd/normalcdf), we find:

$$P(\text{Sold}) \approx 0.920968\dots$$

steps.title: Find probability of recycling
steps.content:
A box is recycled if it is **not** sold. To find the probability that a box is recycled, we use the complement rule:

$$\begin{aligned} P(\text{Recycle}) &= 1 - P(\text{Sold}) \\ &= 1 - 0.920968\dots \\ &= 0.079031\dots \end{aligned}$$

This represents the proportion of the 1500 daily boxes that the manufacturer expects to recycle.

steps.title: Calculate expected daily cost
steps.content:
To find the total expected cost, we multiply the total number of boxes produced by the probability of a box being recycled and the cost per recycled box.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Cost} &= 1500 \times 0.079031\dots \times 0.16 \\ \text{Expected Cost} &\approx \$18.9676\dots \end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Expected Cost} = \$19.0$$

steps.title: Define the Normal Model
steps.content:
First, let's define the random variable $W$ as the weight of a cereal box in grams. The question states that weights follow a normal distribution:

$$W \sim N(502, 2^2)$$

Where the mean $\mu = 502$ and the standard deviation $\sigma = 2$.

highlights.text: normally distributed with a mean of 502 grams and a standard deviation of 2 grams

steps.title: Identify Recycling Conditions
steps.content:
A box is recycled if its weight is **not** between $497.5$ g and $505$ g. Using the **Direct Tail Summation** approach, we need to find the probability of a box falling into either of these two "tail" regions:

1. The lower tail: $W < 497.5$
2. The upper tail: $W > 505$

highlights.text: recycles any box of cereal with a weight not between 497.5 grams and 505 grams

steps.title: Calculate Tail Probabilities
steps.content:
Using a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC), we find the probability for each tail:

- Lower tail: $P(W < 497.5) \approx 0.012224...$
- Upper tail: $P(W > 505) \approx 0.066807...$

Adding these together gives the total probability that a box is recycled ($P_{\text{recycle}}$):

$$P_{\text{recycle}} = 0.012224 + 0.066807 = 0.079031...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions -> Normal -> Cumulative. For the first calculation, set Lower: -1E99, Upper: 497.5, μ: 502, σ: 2. For the second, set Lower: 505, Upper: 1E99.

ti84.instructions: For the lower tail, use 2nd -> DISTR -> 2:normalcdf(-1E99, 497.5, 502, 2). For the upper tail, use normalcdf(505, 1E99, 502, 2).

desmos.instructions: Type normaldist(502, 2).cdf(max: 497.5) and normaldist(502, 2).cdf(min: 505). Sum the results.

calculator.description: Calculate the probabilities for the lower and upper tails of the normal distribution.

steps.title: Calculate Expected Daily Cost
steps.content:
To find the expected daily cost, we use the formula for expected value from the data booklet:

$$\mathrm{E}(X) = np$$

Here, $n = 1500$ (total boxes) and $p = 0.079031\dots$ (probability of recycling). Finally, we multiply the expected number of recycled boxes by the recycling cost per box ($0.16$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Cost} &= 1500 \times 0.079031\dots \times 0.16 \\ \text{Expected Cost} &\approx \$18.9676\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the multiplication: 1500 * 0.07903167 * 0.16.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1500 * Ans * 0.16 (where Ans is the sum of the probabilities).

calculator.description: Multiply the total boxes, the combined probability, and the unit cost.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, the expected daily recycling cost is:

$$\$19.0$$

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content:
Hi there! To find the expected daily recycling cost, we'll use a step-by-step approach. First, we'll calculate how many boxes we expect to **sell** and subtract that from the total to see how many are left for recycling. 

We are given that the weights follow a normal distribution with:
- Mean $\mu = 502$ g
- Standard deviation $\sigma = 2$ g

highlights.text: mean of 502 grams and a standard deviation of 2 grams

steps.title: Calculate probability of sale
steps.content:
A box is sold if its weight is between $497.5$ g and $505$ g. We can find this probability using the normal cumulative distribution function (normalCDF) on a graphing calculator.

Let $X$ be the weight of a cereal box. We want to find:
$$P(497.5 \leq X \leq 505)$$

Using the calculator, we find:
$$P(\text{sold}) \approx 0.920968...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, choose Distributions, then Normal, and select Ncd (Normal Cumulative Distribution). Enter Lower: 497.5, Upper: 505, σ: 2, μ: 502.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd, then VARS. Select 2:normalcdf. Enter lower: 497.5, upper: 505, μ: 502, σ: 2. Highlight Paste and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(502, 2)' and click the 'Find Cumulative Probability (CDF)' checkbox. Enter 497.5 for the Min and 505 for the Max.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability that a box falls within the saleable weight range.

highlights.text: weight between 497.5 grams and 505 grams are sold

steps.title: Find expected boxes sold
steps.content:
Now, we multiply this probability by the total number of boxes produced daily ($1500$) to find the expected number of boxes sold.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Sold} &= 1500 \times 0.920968\dots \\ \text{Expected Sold} &\approx 1381.452\dots \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate expected recycled boxes
steps.content:
Since every box not sold is recycled, we subtract the number of sold boxes from the total production:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Expected Recycled} &= 1500 - 1381.452\dots \\ \text{Expected Recycled} &\approx 118.548\dots \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: recycles any box of cereal with a weight not between 497.5 grams and 505 grams

steps.title: Calculate total recycling cost
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the expected number of recycled boxes by the cost per box ($0.16) to find the total expected daily cost.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Recycling Cost} &= 118.548\dots \times 0.16 \\ \text{Recycling Cost} &\approx 18.9676\dots \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, we get **$19.0$**.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
From the question, we know the cereal box weights follow a normal distribution. We are given the following values for this specific manufacturer:

- Mean $(\mu) = 350$ grams
- Standard deviation $(\sigma) = 1.8$ grams

This falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability** in your syllabus. Specifically, we are looking for a boundary value $(w)$ given a probability, which requires the **Inverse Normal** function.

steps.title: Define the probability area
steps.content:
The manufacturer sells all boxes *above* a minimum weight $w$. Since they sell $97\%$ of the boxes, we can write this as a probability statement:

$$P(X > w) = 0.97$$

Most graphing calculators require the area to the **left** of the value to perform the inverse normal calculation. Since the total area under the curve is $1$, we calculate the left-tail area:

$$\text{Area to the left} = 1 - 0.97 = 0.03$$

highlights.text: sell 97% of the cereal boxes produced

steps.title: Calculate using GDC
steps.content: Now, we use the Inverse Normal function on the calculator. We input our calculated area, the mean, and the standard deviation.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions -> Normal -> Inverse Normal. Input: Area = 0.03, SD (σ) = 1.8, Mean (μ) = 350.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '3:invNorm('. Enter: Area: 0.03, μ: 350, σ: 1.8, Tail: LEFT. Press 'Paste' and [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the minimum weight w using the inverse normal distribution function.

steps.title: State final answer

The calculator gives us a value of $346.614\dots$. 

In IB exams, we usually round our final answer to 3 significant figures unless stated otherwise.

$$w \approx 347 \text{ grams}$$

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters
steps.content:
From the problem, we know the weights of the cereal boxes for this manufacturer follow a normal distribution. We are given the following parameters:

- Mean $(\mu) = 350$ grams
- Standard deviation $(\sigma) = 1.8$ grams

We need to find the minimum weight $w$ such that 97% of the boxes are sold.

steps.title: Determine cumulative probability
steps.content:
The manufacturer sells all boxes that are *above* weight $w$. If they sell 97% of the boxes, this means the area to the right of $w$ on the normal curve is $0.97$.

To find the $z$-score, we usually need the cumulative probability (the area to the left). Since the total area under the curve is 1:

$$P(X < w) = 1 - 0.97 = 0.03$$

So, $w$ is the 3rd percentile of the distribution.

highlights.text: sell 97% of the cereal boxes produced

steps.title: Find the z-score
steps.content:
Now we find the $z$-score corresponding to a cumulative probability of $0.03$. This is the number of standard deviations $w$ is away from the mean.

Using the inverse normal function on your calculator:

$$z \approx -1.88079...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Normal, and Inverse Normal. Set the area to 0.03, μ to 0, and σ to 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'invNorm(', enter Area: 0.03, μ: 0, σ: 1, and select Paste.

desmos.instructions: Type 'inverseNormal(normaldist(0, 1), 0.03)' into a cell.

calculator.description: Find the z-score for a cumulative probability of 0.03.

steps.title: Apply standardization formula
steps.content:
The standardization formula relates the actual value ($w$), the mean ($\mu$), and the standard deviation ($\sigma$) to the $z$-score:

$$z = \frac{w - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Rearranging to solve for $w$:

$$\begin{aligned} w &= \mu + z \cdot \sigma \\ w &= 350 + (-1.88079...) \cdot 1.8 \\ w &\approx 346.614... \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding the result to 3 significant figures, we find the minimum weight required for a box to be sold.

$$w = 347 \text{ grams}$$

parts.content:
In a coffee shop, the time it takes to serve a customer can be modelled by a normal distribution with a mean of 1.5 minutes and a standard deviation of 0.4 minutes.

Two customers enter the shop together. They are served one at a time.

Find the probability that the total time taken to serve both customers will be less than 4 minutes.

Clearly state any assumptions you have made.
parts.markscheme:
• Find the mean and variance of the total time $T$: $E(T) = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$ $\text{Var}(T) = 0.4^2 + 0.4^2 = 0.32$ M1 A1
  • State the distribution of $T$: $T \sim N(3, 0.32)$ A1
  • Attempt to find the probability $P(T < 4)$: Using GDC or standardization $Z = \frac{4 - 3}{\sqrt{0.32}} \approx 1.768$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $P(T < 4) = 0.961$ ($0.96145\dots$) A1
  • State the assumption made: The service times for the two customers are independent. R1
6 marks total

steps.title: Define the total time
steps.content:
Let $X_1$ be the time taken to serve the first customer and $X_2$ be the time taken to serve the second. 

We are looking for the total time $T = X_1 + X_2$. Since both $X_1$ and $X_2$ follow a normal distribution, their sum $T$ also follows a normal distribution.

highlights.text: total time taken to serve both customers

steps.title: Calculate the total mean
steps.content:
The expected value (mean) of the sum of two random variables is the sum of their individual means:

$$\begin{aligned} E(T) &= E(X_1) + E(X_2) \\ &= 1.5 + 1.5 \\ &= 3 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the total variance
steps.content:
To find the variance of the sum, we add the individual variances. First, find the variance of one customer ($Var = \sigma^2$):

$$Var(X) = 0.4^2 = 0.16$$

Now, sum them to find the variance of $T$:

$$\begin{aligned} Var(T) &= Var(X_1) + Var(X_2) \\ &= 0.16 + 0.16 \\ &= 0.32 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the distribution and assumption
steps.content:
For the variance sum rule to work ($Var(A+B) = Var(A) + Var(B)$), we must **assume that the service times for the two customers are independent**. 

The distribution of the total time is:
$$T \sim N(3, 0.32)$$ 

Note that the standard deviation of $T$ is $\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.32} \approx 0.566$.

highlights.text: Clearly state any assumptions you have made

steps.title: Calculate probability with GDC
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that the total time is less than 4 minutes, $P(T < 4)$. 

Using the Normal Cumulative Density Function (Normal CDF) on your calculator with:
- Lower bound: $-10$ (or $-\infty$)
- Upper bound: $4$
- Mean ($\mu$): $3$
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$): $\sqrt{0.32}$

$$P(T < 4) \approx 0.961$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distributions, then Normal, then Ncd. Set the Lower bound to a very small number, Upper bound to 4, σ to √(0.32), and μ to 3.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select '2:normalcdf('. Enter the values as follows: lower:-1E99, upper:4, μ:3, σ:√(0.32).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(3, sqrt(0.32)).cdf(max=4)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the normal cumulative probability for the total time.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
To calculate the total time for two customers, we must assume that the time it takes to serve the first customer does not affect the time it takes to serve the second. In statistical terms, we assume the service times for the two customers are **independent** random variables.

This allows us to sum their means and variances directly.

steps.title: Find the total mean
steps.content:
Let $X_1$ and $X_2$ represent the service times for the two customers, and $T$ represent the total time: $T = X_1 + X_2$.

The mean of the total time is the sum of the individual means:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{E}(T) &= \text{E}(X_1) + \text{E}(X_2) \\ \text{E}(T) &= 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \text{ minutes} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the total variance
steps.content:
Since the times are independent, the variance of the total time is the sum of the individual variances. 

First, find the variance of one customer (the square of the standard deviation):

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = 0.4^2 = 0.16$$

Now, sum them for both customers:

$$\text{Var}(T) = 0.16 + 0.16 = 0.32$$

The standard deviation of the total time, $\sigma_T$, is the square root of this variance:

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{0.32} \approx 0.5657$$

steps.title: Calculate the Z-score
steps.content:
We want to find the probability that the total time is less than 4 minutes ($P(T < 4)$). To use the standard normal distribution, we convert the value $T = 4$ into a Z-score using the formula:

$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting our values for the total time:

$$\begin{aligned} Z &= \frac{4 - 3}{\sqrt{0.32}} \\ Z &= \frac{1}{0.56568...} \\ Z &\approx 1.768 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the probability
steps.content:
Now we find the probability that a standard normal variable is less than $1.768$:

$$P(T < 4) = P(Z < 1.768)$$

Using a GDC or standard normal tables, we find:
$$P(Z < 1.768) \approx 0.96145...$$

Rounding to three significant figures, the probability is **0.961**.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Statistics menu, select Distribution -> Normal -> Ncd. Enter: Lower: -9999, Upper: 1.7677, σ: 1, μ: 0.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 'normalcdf('. Enter the following: lower: -1E99, upper: 1.7677, μ: 0, σ: 1.

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(0, 1)' in one line, then use the 'Cumulative Probability' checkbox and set the upper bound to 1.7677.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability using the Normal Cumulative Distribution function.

steps.title: Define the sample mean
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Sample Mean Distribution** approach, we first define the average service time for the two customers. Let $X$ represent the service time for one customer, where $X \sim N(1.5, 0.4^2)$. 

For a sample of $n = 2$ customers, the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is also normally distributed. According to the **Central Limit Theorem** (or rules for linear combinations of normal variables), the parameters for the sample mean distribution are:

- The mean of the sample mean is the same as the population mean: $\mu_{\bar{x}} = \mu = 1.5$
- The standard deviation of the sample mean (often called the standard error) is: $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}$

steps.title: Translate the total time
steps.content:
The question asks for the probability that the **total time** ($T$) is less than 4 minutes. We can convert this condition into a condition about the **average time** ($\bar{X}$).

Since $T = X_1 + X_2$, the average time is $\bar{X} = \frac{T}{2}$. 

Therefore, the condition $T < 4$ is equivalent to:

$$\begin{aligned} \bar{X} &< \frac{4}{2} \\ \bar{X} &< 2 \end{aligned}$$

We are now looking for $P(\bar{X} < 2)$ where $\bar{X} \sim N\left(1.5, \left(\frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\right)$.

highlights.text: total time taken to serve both customers will be less than 4 minutes

steps.title: State the required assumption
steps.content:
For the distribution of the sample mean to be valid in this way, we must make a key assumption.

**Assumption:** The service times for the two customers are **independent**. This means the time taken to serve the first customer does not affect the time taken to serve the second customer.

highlights.text: Clearly state any assumptions you have made

steps.title: Calculate the probability
steps.content:
Now we use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to find $P(\bar{X} < 2)$. 

We enter the following parameters into the normal cumulative distribution function:
- Lower bound: $-\infty$ (or a very small number like $-10^9$)
- Upper bound: $2$
- Mean ($\mu$): $1.5$
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$): $\frac{0.4}{\sqrt{2}}$

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics menu, go to Distributions -> Normal -> Ncd. Enter Lower: -1E99, Upper: 2, σ: 0.4/√(2), μ: 1.5.

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR), select 2:normalcdf(. Enter lower: -1E99, upper: 2, μ: 1.5, σ: 0.4/√(2).

desmos.instructions: Type 'normaldist(1.5, 0.4/sqrt(2)).cdf(-infinity, 2)' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability P(X̄ < 2) with mean 1.5 and standard deviation 0.4/sqrt(2).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$P(\bar{X} < 2) \approx 0.961$$

This means there is approximately a $96.1\%$ chance that the total service time for both customers will be less than 4 minutes.

steps.title: Identify distribution parameters

From the question, we are given that the wolf's age $L$ follows a normal distribution $L \sim N(8, 5)$. 

In standard IB notation $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, this means:

steps.title: Calculate the Z-score

parts.content:
Eight wolves are independently selected at random and their ages recorded.

Find the probability that more than six of these wolves are at least 5 years old.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $X$ be the number of wolves found to be at least 5 years old
  • Recognise binomial distribution: $X \sim B(8, 0.910\dots)$ M1
  • Find probability: $P(X > 6) = 1 - P(X \le 6)$ M1
  • $= 0.843$ A1

3 marks total

Award M1 A0 for finding $P(X \ge 6)$.

steps.title: Find single wolf probability
steps.content:
First, we need to find the probability that a single wolf is at least 5 years old. We are given that the age $L$ follows a normal distribution $L \sim N(8, 5)$, where the mean $\mu = 8$ and the variance $\sigma^2 = 5$ (which means the standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{5}$).

Using the normal cumulative distribution function (normCDF) on your calculator with a lower bound of $5$, an upper bound of $\infty$ (or $10^{99}$), $\mu = 8$, and $\sigma = \sqrt{5}$:

$$p = P(L \ge 5) \approx 0.91008...$$

It is best to keep this full value in your calculator for the next step to ensure accuracy.

gdc.instructions: Navigate to the probability or distribution menu. Select Normal Cumulative Distribution. Input lower bound 5, upper bound 9E99, mean 8, and standard deviation sqrt(5).

steps.title: Define the binomial distribution
steps.content:
Now we consider the group of $n=8$ wolves. Since each wolf is selected independently and has the same probability $p$ of being at least 5 years old, we can model the number of such wolves, $X$, using a Binomial Distribution:

$$X \sim B(8, 0.910...)$$

We want to find the probability that **more than six** wolves satisfy the condition, which is written as $P(X > 6)$. Since $X$ must be a whole number, this is the same as:

$$P(X > 6) = P(X = 7) + P(X = 8)$$

This is covered in **Topic 4.8: Binomial Distribution** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: more than six of these wolves are at least 5 years old

steps.title: Calculate cumulative probability
steps.content:
Graphing calculators usually have a function called `binomCdf` which calculates $P(X \le k)$. To find $P(X > 6)$, we use the complement rule:

$$P(X > 6) = 1 - P(X \le 6)$$

Using the binomial cumulative distribution function on your calculator with $n = 8$, $p \approx 0.91008$, and $k = 6$, we find $P(X \le 6)$. Then subtract this from 1 to get the final result.

Rounding the result to three significant figures, we get:

$$P(X > 6) \approx 0.843$$

In an IB exam, marks are awarded for identifying the binomial distribution ($M1$), correctly setting up the complement probability ($M1$), and providing the final accurate answer ($A1$).

steps.title: Define the success probability
steps.content:
First, we need to find the probability that a single wolf is at least 5 years old. The question states that the age $L$ follows a normal distribution with a mean of 8 and a variance of 5 ($L \sim N(8, 5)$).

We want to find $p = P(L \ge 5)$. 

Using a GDC (Graphing Display Calculator), we use the **Normal Cumulative Distribution** function with:
- Lower bound: $5$
- Upper bound: $\infty$ (or a very large number like $10^{99}$)
- Mean ($\mu$): $8$
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$): $\sqrt{5}$ (approx. $2.236$)

$$p = P(L \ge 5) \approx 0.91014...$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Normal Cdf command in the statistics or distribution menu.

ti84.instructions: Go to [2nd] [VARS] (DISTR) and select 'normalcdf'. Set lower: 5, upper: 1E99, μ: 8, σ: √(5).

desmos.instructions: Use the normal distribution function with mean 8 and standard deviation sqrt(5), then find the cumulative probability from 5.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability p using the normal distribution.

steps.title: Identify the Binomial distribution
steps.content:
Next, we consider a sample of 8 wolves. Since each wolf is chosen independently, the number of wolves that are at least 5 years old, $X$, follows a Binomial distribution.

$$X \sim B(n, p)$$

Where:
- $n = 8$ (the number of trials)
- $p \approx 0.91014$ (the probability of success found in the previous step)

We are looking for the probability that **more than six** wolves are at least 5 years old, which is $P(X > 6)$. Since $X$ can only be whole numbers, this means we need the sum of the probabilities for exactly 7 and exactly 8 wolves:

$$P(X > 6) = P(X = 7) + P(X = 8)$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Binomial Pdf function for values 7 and 8.

ti84.instructions: Use [2nd] [VARS] 'binompdf'. Calculate for x=7 and x=8 with n=8 and p=0.91014.

desmos.instructions: Use the binomial distribution function and find the probability for specific points.

calculator.description: Calculate individual binomial probabilities for X=7 and X=8.

steps.title: Sum the probabilities
steps.content:
Finally, we add these individual probabilities together to find the total probability of having more than 6 wolves.

$$\begin{aligned} P(X > 6) &= P(X = 7) + P(X = 8) \\ &= 0.36952... + 0.47319... \\ &\approx 0.8427 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get **0.843**.

parts.content: Find the probability that a randomly chosen male bird weighs between $4.75$ kg and $4.85$ kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate the probability: $P(4.75 < X < 4.85) = 0.197$ A1

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the distribution parameters
steps.content:
First, we identify the characteristics of the male bird weights, $X$. The problem states that $X$ is normally distributed. From the information provided, we can identify the mean ($\mu$) and the standard deviation ($\sigma$):

$$\mu = 4.8 \text{ kg}$$

$$\sigma = 0.2 \text{ kg}$$

This falls under **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**, specifically the study of the Normal Distribution (SL 4.9).

highlights.text: normally distributed with mean 4.8 kg and standard deviation 0.2 kg

steps.title: Define the probability interval
steps.content:
We are looking for the probability that a male bird's weight falls between $4.75$ kg and $4.85$ kg. We can write this mathematically as:

$$P(4.75 < X < 4.85)$$

On a normal distribution curve, this represents the area under the curve between these two $x$-values.

steps.title: Use your GDC
steps.content:
Since this is a GDC-focused approach, we will use the **Normal Cumulative Distribution Function (normCDF)**. 

We need to input our four values:
- **Lower bound:** $4.75$
- **Upper bound:** $4.85$
- **Mean** ($\mu$): $4.8$
- **Standard deviation** ($\sigma$): $0.2$

Your calculator will integrate the normal distribution function between these bounds to find the probability.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a result of approximately $0.197412...$. 

In IB Mathematics, we generally round our final answers to **three significant figures** unless otherwise specified. 

$$P(4.75 < X < 4.85) \approx 0.197$$

steps.title: Identify the parameters
steps.content:
First, we look at the information given for the male birds. We know that the weights ($X$) follow a normal distribution, and we are interested in a specific range.

From the problem, we have:
- Mean ($\mu$) = $4.8$ kg
- Standard deviation ($\sigma$) = $0.2$ kg

We want to find the probability: 

$$P(4.75 < X < 4.85)$$

highlights.text: mean 4.8 kg and standard deviation 0.2 kg

steps.title: Calculate the Z-scores
steps.content:
To use the standard normal distribution approach, we need to transform our weight boundaries into $z$-scores using the standardization formula:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Let's calculate the $z$-scores for our two boundaries:

For the lower boundary ($x = 4.75$):
$$z_1 = \frac{4.75 - 4.8}{0.2} = -0.25$$

For the upper boundary ($x = 4.85$):
$$z_2 = \frac{4.85 - 4.8}{0.2} = 0.25$$

steps.title: Determine the standardized probability
steps.content:
Our original question is now converted into finding the area under the standard normal curve ($Z \sim N(0, 1)$) between our two calculated $z$-scores:

$$P(-0.25 < Z < 0.25)$$

You can find this value using the Normal Cumulative Distribution (Normal Cdf) function on your calculator.

gdc.instructions: In the Statistics menu, choose DIST -> NORM -> Ncd. Set the Lower boundary to -0.25, the Upper to 0.25, σ to 1, and μ to 0.

calculator.description: Calculate the area under the standard normal curve between -0.25 and 0.25.

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find that the probability is approximately $0.197412\dots$. 

In the IB, we usually round final answers to 3 significant figures unless stated otherwise:

$$P = 0.197$$

parts.content: Find the probability that the weight of a randomly chosen male bird is more than twice the weight of a randomly chosen female bird.
parts.markscheme:
• Consider the random variable $X - 2Y$ M1
  • Calculate the expectation: $E(X - 2Y) = 4.8 - 2(2.7) = -0.6$ A1
  • Calculate the variance: $\text{Var}(X - 2Y) = \text{Var}(X) + 4\text{Var}(Y) = 0.2^2 + 4(0.15^2)$ M1
  • Obtain the variance value: $\text{Var}(X - 2Y) = 0.13$ A1

So $X - 2Y \sim N(-0.6, 0.13)$

  • Set up the probability $P(X - 2Y > 0)$ M1
  • Find the probability: $0.0480$ A1

6 marks total

steps.title: Define the linear combination
steps.content:
To find the probability that the male weight ($X$) is more than twice the female weight ($Y$), we define the inequality $X > 2Y$. 

This is equivalent to finding $P(X - 2Y > 0)$. Let's define a new normal random variable $W$ representing this linear combination:

$$W = X - 2Y$$

highlights.text: weight of a randomly chosen male bird is more than twice the weight of a randomly chosen female bird

steps.title: Calculate the expected value
steps.content:
The mean (expected value) of a linear combination of random variables is found by applying the same combination to their individual means:

$$E(W) = E(X) - 2E(Y)$$

Substituting the given values:

$$E(W) = 4.8 - 2(2.7) = -0.6$$

steps.title: Calculate the variance
steps.content:
For independent variables, the variance of $aX + bY$ is $a^2 \operatorname{Var}(X) + b^2 \operatorname{Var}(Y)$. For $W = X - 2Y$, the coefficients are $1$ and $-2$:

$$\operatorname{Var}(W) = 1^2 \operatorname{Var}(X) + (-2)^2 \operatorname{Var}(Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + 4 \operatorname{Var}(Y)$$

Using the given standard deviations ($0.2$ and $0.15$):

$$\operatorname{Var}(W) = 0.2^2 + 4(0.15^2) = 0.04 + 0.09 = 0.13$$

steps.title: Define the resulting distribution
steps.content:
Since $X$ and $Y$ are normally distributed, $W$ is also normally distributed. We now have the parameters:

$$W \sim N(-0.6, 0.13)$$

To find the final probability, we need to calculate $P(W > 0)$. Note that the standard deviation for our calculator is:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.13}$$

steps.title: Solve using technology
steps.content:
Using a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) with the Normal Cumulative Distribution function, we calculate the area from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$P(W > 0) \approx 0.0480$$

This is the probability that a male's weight is more than twice a female's weight.

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd -> VARS. Choose 2: normalcdf(. Set lower: 0, upper: 1E99, μ: -0.6, σ: √(0.13). Select Paste and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type normaldist(-0.6, sqrt(0.13)).cdf(0, infinity) into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the probability P(W > 0) where W is normally distributed with mean -0.6 and variance 0.13.

parts.content: Two randomly chosen male birds and three randomly chosen female birds are placed on a weighing machine that has a weight limit of $18$ kg. Find the probability that the total weight of these five birds is greater than the weight limit.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $W = X_1 + X_2 + Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3$ be the total weight.
  • Calculate the expectation: $E(W) = 2E(X) + 3E(Y) = 17.7$ A1
  • Calculate the variance: $\text{Var}(W) = 2\text{Var}(X) + 3\text{Var}(Y)$ M1
  • Obtain the variance value: $\text{Var}(W) = 0.1475$ A1

So $W \sim N(17.7, 0.1475)$

  • Find the probability: $P(W > 18) = 0.217$ A1

4 marks total

steps.title: Define the total weight variable
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first define a new random variable $W$ representing the total weight of the five birds. Since there are two male birds ($X_1, X_2$) and three female birds ($Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$), we can write:

$$W = X_1 + X_2 + Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3$$

From the question, we know the individual distributions are:
- Male ($X$): $\mu_X = 4.8$, $\sigma_X = 0.2$
- Female ($Y$): $\mu_Y = 2.7$, $\sigma_Y = 0.15$

This uses concepts from **Topic 4: Statistics and Probability**, specifically linear combinations of random variables.

highlights.text: mean 4.8 kg and standard deviation 0.2 kg
mean 2.7 kg and standard deviation 0.15 kg

steps.title: Calculate the expected total weight
steps.content:
The expected value of a sum of random variables is the sum of their individual expected values. For $W$, the calculation is:

$$E(W) = 2E(X) + 3E(Y)$$

$$E(W) = 2(4.8) + 3(2.7)$$

$$E(W) = 9.6 + 8.1$$

$$E(W) = 17.7 \text{ kg}$$

This gives us the mean of our new normal distribution.

steps.title: Calculate the total variance
steps.content:
Since the birds are chosen randomly, we assume their weights are independent. For independent variables, the variance of the sum is the sum of the variances. 

Remember: $\operatorname{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$. 

$$\operatorname{Var}(W) = 2 \operatorname{Var}(X) + 3 \operatorname{Var}(Y)$$

$$\operatorname{Var}(W) = 2(0.2^2) + 3(0.15^2)$$

$$\operatorname{Var}(W) = 0.08 + 0.0675 = 0.1475$$

So, the total weight $W$ follows a normal distribution: $W \sim N(17.7, 0.1475)$.

steps.title: Calculate probability using GDC
steps.content:
We need to find the probability that the total weight exceeds the limit of $18$ kg, which is $\text{P}(W > 18)$. 

We use the **Normal Cumulative Distribution Function (Normal CDF)** on the calculator with the following parameters:
- $\text{Lower bound} = 18$
- $\text{Upper bound} = \infty$ (use a large number like $10^{99}$)
- $\mu = 17.7$
- $\sigma = \sqrt{0.1475}$

gdc.instructions:

2. Select Distributions -> Normal -> Normal Cdf.
3. Enter Lower: 18, Upper: 1E99, Mean: 17.7, Standard Deviation: √(0.1475).

4. Highlight 'Paste' and press [ENTER] twice.

2. The result will appear at the bottom of the expression bar.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$\text{P}(W > 18) \approx 0.217$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for $3$ significant figures.

steps.title: Define the total weight variable
steps.content:
Let $W$ be the total weight of the five birds. We are given that there are two male birds ($X_1, X_2$) and three female birds ($Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$). 

$$W = X_1 + X_2 + Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3$$

steps.title: Calculate the total mean
steps.content:
First, we find the expected value (mean) of the total weight $W$. For independent random variables, the mean of the sum is the sum of the means.

From the question, we know:
- Mean male weight $E(X) = 4.8$ kg
- Mean female weight $E(Y) = 2.7$ kg

$$\begin{aligned} E(W) &= 2E(X) + 3E(Y) \\ E(W) &= 2(4.8) + 3(2.7) \\ E(W) &= 9.6 + 8.1 \\ E(W) &= 17.7 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the total variance
steps.content:
Next, we find the variance of $W$. Because the birds are chosen randomly (and thus are independent), the variance of the sum is the sum of the variances.

From the question:
- Standard deviation of males $\sigma_X = 0.2$, so $\text{Var}(X) = 0.2^2$
- Standard deviation of females $\sigma_Y = 0.15$, so $\text{Var}(Y) = 0.15^2$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(W) &= 2\text{Var}(X) + 3\text{Var}(Y) \\ \text{Var}(W) &= 2(0.2^2) + 3(0.15^2) \\ \text{Var}(W) &= 0.08 + 0.0675 \\ \text{Var}(W) &= 0.1475 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Establish the distribution of W
steps.content:
The sum of independent normal variables is also normally distributed. Thus:

$$W \sim N(17.7, 0.1475)$$

To standardize, we need the standard deviation of $W$ ($\sigma_W$):

$$\sigma_W = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.38406...$$

steps.title: Calculate the z-score
steps.content:
We want to find the probability that the total weight exceeds the limit of $18$ kg: $P(W > 18)$. 

We transform the limit into a z-score using the formula $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$:

$$z = \frac{18 - 17.7}{\sqrt{0.1475}}$$

$$z = \frac{0.3}{0.38406\dots}$$

$$z \approx 0.7811$$

steps.title: Determine the final probability
steps.content: Using the standard normal distribution $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, we find $P(Z > 0.7811)$.

Lower: 0.7811
Upper: 9.99E99
σ: 1
μ: 0

calculator.description: Calculate the probability that the standardized weight is greater than 0.7811.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The probability that the total weight is greater than $18$ kg is approximately:

$$P(W > 18) \approx 0.217$$

parts.content: Find the probability that a melon selected at random will have a mass greater than $3.0 \text{ kg}$.
parts.markscheme:

  • Identify the probability calculation: $P(X > 3.0)$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $0.212$ ($0.2118\dots$) A1

parts.content: Find the probability that two melons picked at random and independently will both have a mass greater than $3.0 \text{ kg}$.

• Multiply probabilities for independent events: $0.2118\dots \times 0.2118\dots$ M1

parts.content: Find the probability that two melons picked at random and independently have a total mass greater than $6.0 \text{ kg}$.

  • State the mean of $T$: $\text{E}(T) = 5.2 \text{ kg}$ A1
  • Calculate the variance of $T$: $\text{Var}(T) = 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 = 0.5\text{ kg}^2$ M1 A1
  • Recognize distribution $T \sim \text{N}(5.2, 0.5)$, where the second parameter is the variance (in $\text{kg}^2$)

parts.content:
One year, due to favourable weather conditions, it is thought that the mean mass of the melons has increased.

The owner of the farm decides to take a random sample of $16$ melons to test this hypothesis at the $5\%$ significance level, assuming the standard deviation of the masses of the melons has not changed.

Write down the null and alternative hypotheses for the test.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $\mu$ be the mean mass of the melons produced by the farm
  • State the hypotheses: $\text{H}_0: \mu = 2.6 \text{ kg}$, $\text{H}_1: \mu > 2.6 \text{ kg}$ A1

1 mark total

Accept equivalent text statements if $2.6 \text{ kg}$ is explicitly included.

parts.content: Find the critical region for this test.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify distribution of sample mean under $\text{H}_0$: $\bar{X} \sim \text{N}\left(2.6 \text{ kg}, \frac{0.5^2}{16}\text{ kg}^2\right)$ A1
  • Set up probability statement for critical region: $P(\bar{X} > a) = 0.05$ M1
  • Find the critical value: $a = 2.81 \text{ kg}$ ($2.8056\dots \text{ kg}$) A1
  • State the critical region: $\bar{X} > 2.81 \text{ kg}$ A1

4 marks total

parts.content:
Unknown to the farmer, the favourable weather conditions have led to all the melons having $10\%$ greater mass than the model described above.

Find the mean and standard deviation of the mass of the melons for this year.
parts.markscheme:
• Let $W$ represent the new mass of the melons
  • Calculate the new mean: $\text{E}(W) = 1.1 \times 2.6 = 2.86$ A1
  • Calculate the new standard deviation: $\text{SD}(W) = 1.1 \times 0.5$ M1
  • Obtain the standard deviation: $0.55$ A1

3 marks total

Award M1A0 for variance calculation error: $\text{Var}(W) = 1.1^2 \times 0.5^2 = 0.3025$.

parts.content: Find the probability of a Type II error in the owner’s test.
parts.markscheme:
• Identify definition of Type II error: $P(\text{fail to reject } \text{H}_0 \mid \text{H}_1 \text{ is true})$
  • Substitute values: $P\left(\bar{X} < 2.81 \mid \mu = 2.86, \sigma = \frac{0.55}{4}\right)$ M1
  • Calculate the probability: $0.346$ ($0.3462\dots$) A1

2 marks total

Accept $0.358$ from use of the three-figure answer to part (e).
